comments.text:
let me tell you,  i was a Marine platoon commander in Vietnam 1968-1969.  Hard year.  Lost too many good Marines killed and wounded. 

 We operated in different types of terrain that included sandy pine forests near the coast, rice paddies, elephant grass, jungle, and the mountains.  It was physically and mentally exhausting.  It was stinking hot in the dry season and it rained constantly during the monsoon season.

  I was a company commander in Hawaii 1975-1976.  Hawaii didn't have a jungle course in those days, rather it was OJT taking the company on training exercises on both Oahu and the Big Island.  I can confirm that the terrain really sucked in both places, especially keeping control of a rifle company.  

I did nearly freeze to death at the Army's Arctic Warfare Course at Ft Greely, Alaska in 1977 while I was the 1st Marine Brigade G-4 Operations Officer because one of our contingency plans was in the event of another Korean War.  The commanding general wanted me to advise him as to how to prepare for that mission in the event we had to deploy to Korea during the winter.  So he sent me to the Arctic Warfare Course.

I also spent two years in Latin America supporting a host nation's counter-insurgency effort.  I wasn't as dangerous as Vietnam, but it was dangerous work.  It was a designated combat zone and so I received combat pay and fitness reports.  The country also had some serious terrain challenges.

Retired after 21 years.  Greatest honor in my life was leading Marines, especially in combat.

I know howto help these people....i know how to handle it, i will tell you

The biggest mass grave they found was about 15,000 of the Polish elite who were taken into the Katyn Forest and executed over a period of a couple weeks during the Spring of 1940. The Germans invited neutral press and observers plus the International Committee of the Red Cross as well as Allied officers from German POW camps along with delegations and press from countries allied with Germany to witness and take part in the exhumations. 
The Allied high command knew for a fact that the Soviets were guilty however they still blamed the Germans for it at the farcical kangaroo court of Nuremburg. How horrible would it have looked for the Western Allies if their citizens found out that Churchill and FDR agreed to hand over Poland to the man who ordered the very best and brightest Poles to be systematically rounded up, imprisoned, tortured and then executed? The hypocrisy!
There is also the matter of General Sikorski, leader of the Polish military and government in exile. Sikorski had been asking Stalin, ever since the Western Allies became allied with the USSR, to release the Polish POW's in the Soviet Union. Stalin kept blowing Sikorski off until the Germans found the mass graves in April 1943. Sikorski demanded answers from Stalin but Stalin continued to blame the Germans then outright ignored Sikorski's inquiries. Sikorski then, just 2 weeks later, was killed in a plane crash off British Gibralter along with his daughter and chiefs of staff. The only survivor was the communist "Czech" pilot who miraculously survived without so much as a scratch. Churchill, Stalin and FDR were worried that Sikorski would pull his troops from the Allied cause against Germany to seek a peace with Germany for a smaller independent Poland in which the Poles would then join the fight against Stalin and communism.

comments.author: @RomanGolubev_A
comments.text:
1. In occupied regions, Russian forces or affiliated authorities promoted summer camps—often positioned on the Black Sea or in Crimea—as safe respites from war. Parents in conflict zones, sometimes desperate and under duress, agreed to send their children for what they thought would be a short, secure break.

Example: In Kozacha Lopan, parents sent their children to the “Medvezhonok” camp near the Russian Black Sea coast for a three-week stay. When the village was retaken by Ukrainian forces, Russian troops had already moved the children—and refused to return them.


2. Occupation and Changing Frontlines

Rapid shifts in control—when Ukrainian forces recaptured territory—left children stranded on the Russian side of newly drawn frontlines. Parents then found themselves physically unable to retrieve them, as crossing into Russia became hazardous or impossible.


3. Coercion and Duress

Parents often acted under immense pressure from occupying forces, bombarded by war, lacking food, shelter, or medical care. These camps were presented as humanitarian or rehabilitative opportunities—but parental consent was far from voluntary.


4. Extended or Indefinite Detention

Many parents believed the camp stays were temporary—weeks at most. Instead, many children were held indefinitely or transferred to other locations, sometimes as far away as Russia’s Far East (e.g., Magadan Oblast), making reunification highly challenging.


5. Obstacles to Retrieval

Russian camp authorities often required in-person pickup, and parents—especially fathers aged 18–60—couldn't cross borders due to conscription rules or filtering risks. Even authorized representatives struggle to retrieve children.

Many years ago my husband was in Afghanistan. We'd been trying to have a child for a while and it wasn't happening. He met a boy who was orphaned and left with his abusive uncle. comments.author: @nightelfuser
comments.text:
Something doesn't make sense. Why would the majority of abducted children be mistreated inside Russian facilities? Isn't that counterproductive if the objective is make them more Russian-friendly? (VICE's claim). If mistreatment did take place like this boy describes here (17:30), those children would grow up hating Russia instead. Also, their lives wouldn't be that different had they remained in war-torn cities, so what would be the point of kidnapping them only to abuse them further? Something doesn't add up here. 

There is definitely some russification taking place. However, it seems illogical that Russians are mistreating those children. There is no benefit in doing that, plus not to mention all of the negative coverage coming from Western media in regards to these alleged abductions. It seems way more reasonable that the children are being treated well, just as if they were Russian, which is a very humane thing to do. I seriously doubt that Ukrainian officials would be just as gentle towards Russian children. Oh wait, we know that they wouldn't because they've been shelling Eastern Ukraine for almost 10 years now. 

Now in regards to the children who were separated from their families, I hope that they are reunited soon. We don't know the situation of many of those children. At least 30% of Ukrainians were ethnic Russians, and spoke Russian as their main language. For this reason, we should go case by case, rather than saying that they're ALL being abducted against their will.

comments.text:
It's nice that they all think asking Russia to stop and sending positive thoughts will work. But the world has never worked that way. It would be nice if we could have it our way and Russia could be a friend and ally and we could all ride off into the sunset together. But the world has never worked that way. Since we can't play the game by our rules, we have to play it by theirs, and that means putting up a goddamned fight. 

We need to draw a line in the sand. Between what we want to do, and what we need to do. If we truly believe in our values, then we need to be willing to stand up for them. Even if we don't *want* to fuel a war in another country, sometimes we *need* to, because it is the right thing to do. Anything less than that would be a complete betrayal of our values, and I feel *that* will be far more destructive than any one war ever could be.

comments.author: @shrimp5093
comments.text:
I'm against sending weapons to Ukraine, not because I think that Russia will just casually go away, or that it's acceptable for Russia invade its neighbor.

My arguments:
1. Russia and the West have been at odds for decades, influence over Ukraine has been at the forefront since the 90s, even Bill Clinton said that the EU would not achieve its goals unless Ukraine was pulled away from Russia.
2. The Russo-Ukrainian war has not stopped despite billions in funding, in fact it has slowed down into a very long and extremely costly war, not just a financially costly war but also one that is also killing dozens of Ukrainians every week, and they're not capable of turning the tide. 
3. The West doesn't understand the relationship between Russia and Ukraine, nor the perpetual war with Russia since Ukraine's founding. Simply funding a country to stop its invader isn't going to help create long lasting peace and a better solution to the poor Russo-Ukrainian relationship.
4.  I think the war has partially been caused by the West funding Ukraine during the civil war in favor of the pro-Western side and government, but the truth is that Ukraine has always been divided on what should be the future of Ukraine.
There is a reason that there was a brutal civil war that ended with many deaths on both sides, and that I think neither Russia, Ukraine, or the West properly managed, and in some areas even chose to ignore. You also don't have to negotiate with an invading force, definitely not, but you also shouldn't have to pay because your government has helped create the environment that lead to the invasion. 
5. This coverage also didn't mention that Germany has been increasing their military spending way beyond what was agreed on after WW2, so a lot of Germans are unhappy about living in a Germany that has a very strong and powerful army again.
Germany is arguably the most important and most powerful country in the EU, as well as having a geographical advantage over all of its neighbors, it holds a lot of power over its neighbors who are all extremely reliant on Germany to function.
6. This will not end with Russia backing down and leaving, but with Russia doing what they do best since the fall of the USSR, attack in waves separated by periods of recovery. This will potentially lead to a very long and drawn out war, that will eventually cause the West to give up on Ukraine eventually, or at least slow down enough to lose what they're trying to achieve.
7. The West is not ready to support Ukraine, they don't have the equipment necessary to help Ukraine fight, because most of the weaponry that Ukraine and Russia use have been dismantled in the West. We were not expecting a war to be fought on land with 20th century equipment, but Ukraine is so big that they are almost entirely reliant on older means of warfare.

comments.text:
TL;DR Until the anti-Ukraine movement does something more than arts and crafts and burning calories, Germany is playing it's parts as a good citizen of the world and can have my respect.

Nice to know (or horrifying, I haven't decided which) that the U.S. has not cornered the market on people who can only think one step ahead in any given moment.  Chess players, these are not.  Ask them a question about what happens after the thing that they are asking for and they'll repeat themselves using different words or deflect.  They haven't thought that far ahead and they don't want to - too much thinking might lead to a conclusion that's not in line with their bias.

Germany is fortunate, IMO, to even have a seat at the table when it comes to deliberations concerning war in Europe.  This is an interesting peek inside - and I'm sure there's a measure of this going on in all NATO countries.  For now, I choose to see Germany in the light of it's governing coalition's actions - and not the words of it's opponents.  

They are taking in the sick, elderly, women and children - remember, Ukrainian men who can fight are not permitted to leave Ukraine.  This is the right thing to do at any time, in any place, no matter who you are or what you believe.  Furthermore and IMHO - the actions and inactions of 20th century German's has saddled their descendants with an extra obligation - and any sacrifice on the part of Germany or it's citizens in taking in refugees is simply fulfillment of the same.

comments.author: @c128stuff
comments.text:
Almost a year later.. here we are with a full scale war between Ukraine and Russia... While the Russian army is much stronger on paper, and can afford losses the Ukrainians can't afford, the military balance between the 2 seems to have dramatically shifted towards Ukraine. 

The why of that is not difficult to understand. Russia with its huge borders can't just move in all its resources without leaving itself highly vulnerable. Also, it has to focus on a wide range of capabilities, and is dealing with very serious corruption issues. Ukraine can totally focus on one thing, keeping out Russia, and can throw the full 100% of its military capabilities on just that. Corruption is also a thing in Ukraine, and not a small thing.. but its not at the insane level it has taken in Russia.. so every monetary unit spent on defense by Ukraine is going to be more effective than when Russia spends exactly the same. 

Ukraine also has more capable command and control (it is shocking to see how disfunctional Russian command and control is), and its president turns out to be a very inspiring wartime leader.

Last but not least, NATO has spent significant effort on training Ukrainian units both with regards to combined operations and asymetric warfare. 

Those things are less obvious from 'paper strength', but are demonstrating how much they matter in reality.

If Russia really really wants to win at all cost, it probably can because it has the numbers to simply keep throwing more resources at the problem, but that will end in the way you describe, an insurgency which will be impossible to control, and it causes very serious weakness for Russia everywhere else.

comments.text: The land bridge to Crimea invasion plan seems a good bet. This would secure a large portion of the northern Black Sea as well as water supplies to a critical piece of strategic geography-the Crimea and the port at Sevastapol. This would also qualify under the 'minor incursion' standard whimsically mused upon by the ever spaced out Biden. Such a 'minor' move would maybe spare Putin from the more draconian regime of sanctions and repercussions while also limiting Russian exposure to insurgency fueled by Western powers. Whatever happens, it is merely one piece of the board which Russia covets going forward, and a prelude to the eventual recovery of all of Ukraine which may take decades. It will also firm up and expand Nato and drive the alliance closer together as the Baltic states will be rightfully alarmed, Poland driven to new heights of paranoia and Turkey filled with fresh suspicions of their old enemies to the north. It will inaugurate a new era beyond the 'post-Cold War era' we've been in since 1991.

comments.author: @anmuloced8673
comments.text:
So there's something I've noted with your geopolitical analysis that has always struck me odd. You point out actions and statements made by nuclear powers that demonstrate their concern for invasive opportunities. The evidence you offer makes these points irrefutable, but personally baffling.

I can understand why a non-nuclear state like Mexico would be concerned about a demonstrated invasion pathway like Vera Cruz. It explains diplomatic ties to places like Cuba, for one. What I don't understand is why countries like China and Russia are concerned about shoring up potential weaknesses in regards to invasion. 

In what scenario does any country start a land invasion from the sea of Azov spearheading to Russian Volvograd without sparking AT LEAST a limited nuclear exchange? Russia can turn any capital city on the planet into a radioactive crater in less than an hour. Why isn't this good enough? What good is a nuclear deterrent if you act (and spend) as if you don't have it?

I just don't understand the strategic thinking there. Are the nuclear powers anticipating a technological innovation that will render the nuclear deterrent ineffective? Are China and Russia just paranoid and socially scarred from the experiences with invasion in WWII? 

I find this boggling. Please help.

comments.author: @brianarps8756
comments.text:
I am not advocating any action, but would not the western political response be much the same in the event of a small advance rather than a big one? The US and its friends would do whatever it could, short of military involvement, to hurt Russia and drive it back, no matter how big the advance, but I do not see why US action would be any more successful in Ukraine than say, than in Afghanistan. How do you rate Russia vs the Taliban? 
The response of the Ukrainian people would vary, in part, on the ethnicity of the individuals concerned and the attitudes of their friends. I do expect that the non-Russian Ukrainians would mostly choose to live elsewhere, but would Romania and Poland close the borders to such refugees, or alternatively accept a few million more refugees into the EU? And I suspect that many ethnic Russians would be happy to be "absorbed into the motherland" as some residents of Crimea have claimed. Some from Crimea mentioned the increased pensions as a factor in their attitude, but many people would still stand on principle despite such inducements. 
So if the benefit of a small advance is no better than a big advance why should not the Russians choose the big advance?
Your point about insurrection is valid. It is amazing how much trouble a gang with a few weapons and a million dollars can make. Russia however saw off the Chechin independents in an unpleasant war, easily bad enough to give one pause.  
Yet if Ukrainians were offered a chance to vote for independence or absorbtion my guess is that the margin would be collectively small. Support for politicians will be low in Ukraine due to the fact that none of them seem able to extract themselves from the current position. But people generally prefer the local to the far away. If the vote was held on a province by province basis, there would be some provinces that would choose Russia, at least, that is my guess from far far away.
At this point Putin could invite the West to accept the vote, or else Russia stays in control of all Ukraine. A difficult decision for the West. No one likes sharing their lunch.
The idea that Putin will act in a restrained manner, widespread in the west, is inconsistent with Chechnya, and being reasonable has its limits. The recent Russian mobilisation showed that action speaks louder than words.
Your characterisation of the Minsk agreement as effectively a set of words to ward off a big advance by Russia and not to be taken seriously implies that the Russians were mugs to have accepted it. Was it wise to tell them that?
I favour negotiation over war because it is generally more effective in generating peaceful outcomes. But the idea that Ukraine can succeed in opposition to Russia given its geographical position is surely contrary to a Geopolitical frame of analysis, let alone common sense. As it is Ukraine is effectively an island in the Black Sea with no friends and an uncertain future.  No-one should want that for them.

comments.author: @pavlozalevskyi
comments.text:
Hi, thanks for the video)
But you are wrong: there are 2 interpretations of the meaning of the word “Ukraine” (the Russian and Ukrainian ones). In Russian (Украина) it means borderland: “U” means “near” and “kraine” — “land”, so “near the main land” — borderland. But in Ukrainian at least for 200 jears it means “Inland” and there is two equal variants of naming this country, that have similar meaning. The more popular one is “Ukraine” (Україна) and the other  one —“Vkraine” (Вкраїна) is more often used in poetry (Taras Shevchenko [cofounder of classical Ukrainian language] has always called his land “Вкраїна”).
“U” or “V” in Ukrainian mean only “in”, “kraine” — “country”, so “in the country” or political “the heart of the country“ [Rus’].
The first time Ukraine is mentioned in “Tale of Bygone Years”, written in ca. 1113 by Nestor and this name is used to call not all of the modern Ukrainian territories, but only those lands that belonged two the only capital of the Rus’ — Kiev. There were several principalities, but only «one capital». That is why Kiev is often called a «mother of all Rus‘ cities”.
In Ukraine we also use word “Okraina” that means borderland for south-west Russian land, where used to live Ukrainians. You can see bough names on the “Map of Eastern Europe” by the Italian Vincenzo Maria Coronelli (1690). The right bank and the left bank with Kiev are marked as VKRAINE ou PAYS DES COSAQUES (Ukraine or the Country of Cossacks); The word OKRAINA (Outskirts) is used in relation to Zadonnya and Ryazan - the Moscow border.

comments.author: @MrPhiltri
comments.text:
I have been following this channel for a while, but this is the first video on a subject, I am fully confident to dispute. Unfortunately, the report was lacking. Please consider my points:
1) The report was published on the 13th, long after investigative reports have examined the date and conclusively shown, that russian warships did in fact fired at ukrainian vessels in international waters. This clear fact is lacking.
2) It is dubious to me, why the motivation for the incident is put on ukraine solely. The arguments made are reasonable, yet not exhaustive. The channel does not investigate, why the russians blocked the strait in the first place, an action, which cannot be done sponteneuosly, but must be plannen.
3) The argument with regards to Poroshenkos popularity has become a standard in most outlets. However, there is very little merit to it. First survey show that a vast majority of urkainians do suspect the proclamation of Martial law to be an act of personal interest for Poro. Hence, his popularity is unlikely to rise dramatically. This is further emphasized by the opposition around Tymoshenko, who was hinting at such developments already in late spring and now feels envigorated. 
4) Lastly, the popularity argument can be made for Putin as well. Why this is missing, I fail to understand.

All in all, the opinion given here is not wrong, but does require a stronger foundation. That said, this of course can be very difficult, if relevant language skills are lacking.

comments.text:
I was just wondering about your assessment of the navigability of the Kerch Straight. You said Panamax vessels transverse the Kerch Straight. Old Panamax vessels have a draft of 12m, new Panamax vessels have a draft of approximately 14m, and the maximum navigable depth of the Kerch Straight is approximately 8m. Even if you could get a vessel through the Kerch Straight, the maximum depth of water in the Sea of Azov is 15m; the average depth is approximately 11m and rapidly diminishes to 7m and much less as you get closer to shore. Delivering a Panamax vessel into the Sea of Azov is an impossible feat as is delivering its cargo to shore. Are you sure you didn't mean a fishing vessel or a shallow draft coal barge a third of the size of a Panamax? Or are you just attempting to create an argument so you can criticize the height of the Bridge? 

Just as I ask most sites that lie; who's paying you for your treachery?

comments.text:
You put a bit too much pressure on reasons Ukraine might have wanted this to happen (which is true) mostly sliding past the elections part which is crucial. Poroshenko wanted to delay the elections by a few months because people are going to get enormous heating bills in February and March due to new gas agreements with Russia. So the chances of his reelection are plummeting even more. If elections were to be held in May those chances would have been better. But he has so little support that the proposed duration of Martial law wasn't approved by the Parliament and was settled on this half-measure. Also if you see the recording from inside the Russian vessel it's quite clear that those people need no provoking to attack. And Ukrainian vessels were laughable as a threat.   
So all in all it looks like some Russian military guys got battle-starved and overzealous, Poroshenko tried to use it to his advantage but mostly failed and now the Ukrainian leadership just trying to squeeze the most of it by dragging the international community in. It is an interesting show of how a minor incident can be used as leverage by both sides on the big politics arena when it had no big reason why it occurred.

This subsonic ASM with 300 km range and a relatively small 160 kg warhead, has managed to hit and sink the pride of the Russian Black Sea fleet - Moskva cruiser. Let's not forget that this 12 500 tonne cruiser was protected by a multiple defensive systems, supposedly the best Russia has in it's arsenal. From the long range anti missile system S-300 F, which is a navalised version of their vaunted S-300 SAM, to the CWIS system with its 2 multi barrel guns that spew out 12 000 rounds per minute. Of course Russia touted these weapons as the best naval protection systems in the world, superior to US Navy's Aegis + SM family of missiles + ESSM + RAM + CWIS with a single multi barrel gun that shoots 4500 rpm.
And last but not least, the Neptune anti ship missile was developed from the old Soviet era system, in a record time after Russian invasion of Crimea in 2014. Ukrainian weapons experts have managed to develop and field the anti ship cruise missile in only 8 years, that managed to sink the ship that was guarded by systems developed by, supposedly, some of the best missile experts in the world?
I predict a significant dip in the interest of the international community, for buying Russian weapon systems.

comments.author: @TrangleC
comments.text:
The 7 dead Russian generals are really perplexing. I might be wrong, but from all I know, there weren't that many dead generals in all of WW2. At least you never read anything about generals being killed in battle in the history books. I only know of three cases of a top level officer being killed in WW2. The first one is that famous Japanese admiral Yamamoto, who was killed in a friendly fire accident when his plane was shot down by Japanese anti air guns, or something like that. The second is Rommel committing suicide and the third one is a German admiral who's name I don't know going down with the battleship Bismarck. 
So only one of those 3 actually died in battle. 
To have 7 generals killed in action a comparably small war like that is really weird.

comments.author: @HooDatDonDar
comments.text:
To all the Russian sympathizers who say Ukraine is nothing without weapons:

They could probably be defeated - that is the material side you count on so much. They would then start the best funded and supported guerrilla movement in the history of the world. That is the spirit side.
Don’t be too sure it would be so easy to crush. The Baltic States kept theirs going for a few years after the war.  Ultimately, Stalin crushed them (I won’t go into how).They had no outside support - the West was rebuilding itself and could not.

Not the case here. I have no doubt Putin could be as ruthless as Stalin, including using chemical weapons, and certain other methods in the occupied land. But there are lots more of these fighters, over more land, and they will get outside support to a degree no one would have thought possible. As for being tough enough to fight an utterly ruthless invader - well, I had my doubts. I do not now.

And while this is all going on, the rest of the world has had the hell scared out of it. Do you think they will not react to the (conventional forces) destruction of Ukraine? With all the atrocities we have seen to an even greater degree? Don’t. There is an excellent chance that not only will current sanctions be increased, but total sanctions will be imposed. China is already coming on board, India likely too, if opinion changes there.

 No buying gas, oil or anything (USA, Canada and , UK already arranging to get subsidized oil and gas to Europe to make up for no Russian gas/oil). No selling Russia anything at all, maybe a few medicines. Overseas funds already frozen, and access to the banking system shut off. If Russia is broke, and has no tech, and turns into a giant version of North Korea, how will it be able to keep the war machine going? North Korea only keeps going with massive subsidies - who will subsidize Russia? Modern armies need modern arms and elec tech. Russia imports a lot of that, which will be over. Even if they can make some at home, how will they pay for it? Conscripting Putin’s broke and starving serfs will give free labor, but materials, knowledge? 

And this is the best scenario for Russia. If the Ukranian state does not go under, this will all be much worse, much sooner. 
How tight a grip does Putin have on domestic unrest, under the conditions I have described? I am genuinely not sure. Inside Russia will be fun to watch - from a distance. 

Clausewitz said that in war, the material was to the spirit as the haft of a sword was to the blade. Meaning, you can do something with only the blade, but a bladeless haft is useless. 

The material you value so highly is the haft. And the Russian troops with their desertion and abandoning equipment don’t show much blade - except against defenseless civilians. Ukraine shows a mighty blade of fighting spirit - with a growing haft. 

Slava Ukraini!

comments.author: @victorfinberg8595
comments.text:
Your videos are chock full of accurate information.

However, there are some additional points worth mentioning.

1) In modern warfare, it's essential to train your troops for small unit flexibility and combined arms procedures. That has been known and applies for many decades, and to a certain extent, even for thousands of years, eg. look at Alexander's army.

2) This new Russian "battalion" contains 7 companies of various sorts, all armoured, so it's really a half-brigade.

3) As you are describing it, very well, I must add, The Russians appear to be using blitzkrieg/echelon operations, except, there are no second and third echelons (aka follow-on forces). This is obviously stupid. It's Market Garden all over again. That was 80 years ago! And that lesson was learned thousands of years ago, at least in some sense.

4) There's nothing new in the Ukrainian operations. The exact same thing has been done many, many times before. Sure, nowadays you can carry around ATGMs, but that's just a detail. 3,000 years ago, you would roll rocks down a mountainside onto troops on the trails below.

5) There is an obvious counter to heavily armed light infantry: artillery. Tell me, are the Russians reverting to heavy use of artillery? (That's a rhetorical question. The Russians have always used artillery heavily.)

Summary: Javelin/Stinger/Bayraktar Oh my.

First and foremost, the American people are tired of spending hundreds of billions of dollars on this war. Without U.S. funding, this conflict would likely have already ended with a Russian victory. That’s why Trump checked Zelensky when it seemed like he was starting to feel entitled to American support or even considering using U.S. media to pressure us. Trump reminded him how things actually work—our aid isn’t guaranteed, and it comes from the hard-earned money of the American people.

Second, Russia has proven to be much weaker than many assumed. If they can’t defeat Ukraine, it’s clear they’d have no chance against the U.S., let alone NATO as a whole. That said, Putin’s desperation could lead to catastrophic outcomes, like the use of nuclear weapons. Nobody wants that scenario—it would be devastating for the entire world.

The U.S. cannot and will not fund this war indefinitely. We’ve just wrapped up decades-long conflicts, and continuing to pour resources into Ukraine isn’t sustainable. If U.S. support ends, Ukraine’s position becomes far more precarious, which would be a terrible outcome for everyone. The only logical solution is to push for negotiations, whether through incentives or diplomatic pressure.

Trump’s approach is often misunderstood because it doesn’t follow the traditional playbook. He listens to what the American people say on the campaign trail and acts accordingly. That’s why so many “experts” struggle to predict his moves—he prioritizes common sense and the concerns of the voters over conventional political strategies.

comments.text:
I think people overthink this. He's doing two things: First, look at the history of US-Russia relations. It went very bad in 1950s and 1960s, then in the early 1970s the heat was at the peak point and America had very big economic problems so they decided to cool it down, then they had detente which ended when Russia invaded Afghanistan in 1979. In 1980s the cold war was becoming serious again and by the end of the decade they were again starting a detente when the Russians got exhausted from Afghanistan and economically, then they had a period of good relations in the 1990s and at the end of the 1990s when the US bombed Serbia and former Warsaw pact countries joined NATO the relations worsened, then after 9/11 they got better, they even signed a NATO-Russia council in 2002 and Russia built a military hospital in Kabul for Americans to use and even allowed them to use Russian bases in central asia to invade Afghanistan, then relations got worse after Baltics joined NATO in 2004 and Rose Revolution in Georgia happened and Orange revolution in Ukraine, it culminated in Georgian war of 2008 which led to better relations during first years of Obama administration, then relations got worse after 2014 with another revolution in Ukraine and Russians annexing Crimea yada yada, then they got to an all time low in 2022-2025 and now a reset again.
THIS IS HAPPENING AGAIN.

Basically since both are thermonuclear powers with thousands of nukes, if they enter a WW3 they'll end the world and nobody wants that, so they decide to reset the relations and then the same thing happens again.
Trump may want Russia to align with him against China, and he may well be able to unalign Russia and China, yes, but it doesn't mean that they won't go back to bad relations once Russians get back on their feet (sort-a) and start to attack their neighbors for security or other reasons again, not that Russians are evil but historically they always tried to create a buffer to deter invasions since their lands are very plain, and they know that Germans and Poles and others are in a similar situation so they will try to do something about that.

THIS IS HAPPENING AGAIN. Vicious circle.

Oh yeah, I started by saying he's doing two things: the second thing is he's breaking the alliance between Russia and China, that's it.

At the beginning of Ukraine's independence, it had the world's third largest nuclear arsenal, 3,000 multi-nuclear warheads, 800,000 troops in the entire Southern Army Group, 10,000 main battle tanks, 11,000 armored vehicles, more than 1,000 fighters, 3 aircraft carriers and nearly 300 combat ships, a huge defense industry of nearly half of the Soviet Union and countless mineral deposits, 50 million population, not today's 26 million, and 25% of the world's black soil was called the granary of Eastern Europe.

comments.author: @Shockedge07
comments.text:
Ukraine are Russia are using the US as a middle man to negotiate peace. Russia is objectively winning at this point. Obviously the terms favor Russia, why tf would they stop fighting otherwise?

Of course they get to keep the Ukrainian territory they occupy; they literally won it through conquest and at a great cost, it's theirs now and someone is going to have to keep fighting if that's going to change. Sorry, but that's not a "conssssion", that's just how war works.

And why would we keep sanctioning them after they do what we ask and stop fighting? Russia has the upper hand and has no incentive to stop the war if the negative consequences for them remain unchanged. They haven't lost the war, so why would they accept penalties for peace?

As for pulling US troops out of eastern NATO countries (which you said wasn't even official yet), well that one is quite significant. But I think it's being interpreted as a signal that the US wouldn't fight Russia if they invaded NATO countries, which is an absolutely ridiculous assertion. In the event of another invation, our response time would be slower and our forces not as prepared, but we'd still be there in a jiffy because we're contractually obligated to defend NATO nations. But permanently stationing our troops isn't a requirement, and I think it would be better interpreted as a sign that, after the deal is made, that we will trust Russia to stay out of Europe for now. Also, stationing troops overseas is expensive and this comes at a time when the US agenda is wholesale expense reduction; so if Russia is playing nice, then costly deterents on our end is unnecessary. But of course I don't blame the host countries for wanting to beef up their own defenses, but that's their responsibility, not ours. 

And as for barring Ukraine from NATO, well that really is throwing Ukraine under the bus. But whatever. What do they do for us? What makes them valuable as an ally worth dying over? As an American soldier who will be on my 2nd 9 month rotation to Poland starting in 2026... I'm personally willing to see Ukraine fall rather than trigger the bear via Ukraine joining NATO and risking WWIII. I don't WANT to see Ukraine fall but God damnit, Ukraine is not our responsibility to defend. Choosing to take on that extremely risky responsibility after all of this would be incredibly stupid. Sorry Ukraine, but after an evaluation of your conflict history, you do not qualify for NATO insurance.

Not reckless deal making.

Not a strategic betrayal. 

Trump was a not a strategic genius, and he has completely surrounded himself with people stupider than himself. 

It’s not worth overthinking this. 

Trump is  the most compromised and criminal man to every reside in the Whitehouse. He is comprehensively indebted to Putin, and he needs somewhere to run to if his plan to die as America’s King instead of going straight to jail post term fails.

He is Putin’s agent in Washington. For all intents and purposes, the US has become a Russian client state under Trump. 

The evidence for this is all the things Trump could do to force Russia to give up that he is not doing to Russia, and all the things Trump is doing to wreck the US and the global order that has so served its interests.  This can only be explained one of two ways which might both be true:
1) trump is a malicious Narcissist incapable of seeing his failures and crimes as his fault, who is so stupid, ignorant, and incompetent, so obsessed with surrounding himself with sycophants, so desperate to avoid the consequences of his actions, so willing to lie, cheat and steal to keep himself going, so unhinged from any sense of morality or sense of duty or integrity that he will take money from anywhere, so unwilling to see himself as anything other than a victim, that he is just a wrecking ball destroying everything. 
Or/and…
2) he has been recruited by Putin as an FSB asset, and is doing Putin’s bidding, giving him everything he wants. 

Both separately explain Trump’s actions and Russian support for him. Together is more probable as it explains why elements that would show up in one scenario but not the other are showing up together. 

At best the US under Trump has become unreliable and transactional and views alliances as an undesirable burden, at worst the US is now being run by Moscow ready to betray allies at the worst possible moment to benefit Moscow. 

Personally I would prepare for both.

comments.text: It turns out, as the situation has transpired in the year since this was published, that 'Russian-speaking' is not the same as 'Russian' (or maybe you'd call it 'ethnic Russian'), and 'ethnic Russian' does not equate with 'I want to be a Russian citizen'. There are those who grow up in the East and speak Russian as their first language who are Ukrainian all the same, with ancestral roots that are very Ukrainian, those who maybe have strong roots in Russia too, and speak Russian, but still consider themselves to be Ukrainian due to living there and wanting to be there so long, for generations even, and even those who may have more recently come from Russia and be 'Russian', but chose to live in Ukraine. Putin's idea that he can impose on any of these groups that they want to be a part of Russia is his mere fantasy that the people on the ground can't speak for themselves but have to fit his imperial ambitions. A fact he doesn't mention, and bans from his country's media, of course, is that as many people from Donetsk and Luhansk fled westwards through the fighting lines into central and western Ukraine to escape this as into Russia. We also know since how hard the Russians make it to flee westwards and how they deliberately push any evacuees into Russia. We also see how happy many Russian speakers are to be liberated from the Russian occupation in Kherson and elsewhere. To this we can add other observations: Putin's claim that Lenin just made this region arbitrarily is not supported by the fact that at the time, though the urban population may have had a lot of Russian speakers (and don't forget what I said above, about how that doesn't make them 'Russians'), the actual majority for the regions, when you included the rural population too, was more Ukrainian, and the surrounding countryside definitely Ukrainian. We also have plenty of Russian speakers evacuating to the west, plenty fighting on the Ukrainian side, and plenty who have escaped from the eastern regions saying they never wanted this 'liberation'. We should also point out that the main justification for this 'protection' has been the idea that Russian was banned, but it wasn't. Even in Lviv it isn't. Lavrov to this day claims that in Kyiv people are attacked for speaking Russian, but a huge amount of the population speak it as their first language, and do so on the street no problem. And we have video material up to this day, throughout the war, of Ukrainians freely speaking Russian in the whole of Ukraine, including among the soldiers, just as we have the video of popular TV shows, such as Savik Shusters 'Svoboda Slova' equally allowing Russian, or Ukraine's version of 'The Voice' having singers singing Russian songs in Russian and talking to the jury in Russian, etc., etc., etc. Even the TV series that made Zelensky famous is 95% in Russian, with him speaking Russian almost all of the time. Of course the statistics about civilian deaths since 2014 in DNR and LNR are severely distorted and misreprested by Russian leaders too, as is any of their 'information' about Nazis in Ukraine. The milked and exaggerated and exploited anything they could in order to fulfil mere imperial ambitions. And they have caused thousands of deaths of those in those republics that they supposedly want to protect to do so.

I'm watching this at the end of January 2022. It is pretty much as I had suspected, even though I did not have the details or even knowledge of this 'New Russia' idea. 

The one thing that makes Putin extremely dangerous is his intelligence. Especially his shrewdness in identifying the weaknesses of the West. Especially, its true lack of cohesion. I honestly believe that Russia did play a large role in influencing the election of Donald J. Trump and in BREXIT. Putin knew that both these highly controversial and therefore divisive events (the Presidency of Trump and BREXIT) would serve to weaken us. The old 'divide and conquer' strategy. Despite all of the talk of 'across the pond', Putin recognises that there has actually been a weakening of relations. In the days of greater closeness, we only needed to say 'Transatlantic'. No need for nonsense references of the Atlantic Ocean to a 'pond'. From everything I have seen , read and analysed I don't think Russia's immediate plan is to take back the whole of Ukraine .
That would be a bridge too far and would bring the whole World down on Russia . A more viable plan is to take the South Eastern part of Ukraine a bit at a time using separatists as a cover for FSB insurgents and connect a land corridor to The Crimea . They talk about the reunification of Ukraine but that's just to fire up the Nationalists to support the war effort . The real objectives at the moment  is economical gain not Political or cultural reunification . If Putin can take much of the coastline of Ukraine 
 then it extends their  oil and gas drilling potential , and inland more  agricultural land etc.  Russian troop build-up could just be a pressure tactic against NATO and a negotiating tool to  allow Russia more time to continue it's incursions into SE Ukraine and  try to scare NATO into  doing nothing about it . I've seen lots of discussions about Putins statement to safe the Russian culture/people on Ukraines soil and EUs help to help democratise Ukraine.

Well actually 2 goals for Putin, second being: keep Ukraine in a conflict (civil war) to negate joining of NATO. As long as Ukraine has undefined borders and/or is in a conflict it cannot join NATO. So in his interest he mustn't win against Kiev only prolongue the war indefinitely, so Amerika loses its interest/burnes enough money.

It was Russian or EU market for them to choose, shifting them in the direction of Europe and undermining Russian influence. Belarus and Ukraine are like puffer states between Russia and Europe, they guarantee Russia some reaction time in case of an attack (also in case of a nuclear strike). To bring them into NATO is not liberating them, it's to bring them into an endless conflict with Russia and the West knows this.

comments.text: This is propaganda, appealing to our State Department of neocon, anti-Russian orientation.   There is no evidence that Russia has intentions to "take-back" Ukraine.    What Russia reasonably will not tolerate is a Ukraine, on its border, that serves as a base for NATO forces.  When the USSR placed its forces in Cuba - in retaliation for US bases in Turkey - the US almost started a war.   One difference is that Ukraine's invitation to NATO is not a retaliation for any threat to Ukraine from Russia.   Russia does not have armed forces on the Ukrainian border.   It has gathered its own forces at least 150km from that border on its own sovereign land.   It did that only in response to Ukraine's threat to Russian-speaking people in the Donbass and Ukraine's failure to carry out its obligations under the 2015 Minsk Agreement that it signed.       I am an American, but I see the belligerence and warlike posture of the USA and see it as just another facet of my government's folly, indeed insanity!

comments.author: @AngloSaks666
comments.text: I remember the talk as if Russia wanted to take the whole of Georgia in 2008, which was really stretching things. Some hawkish ultra-nationalists of course always would like to, and did then, but that wasn't Russia's intention at all, if you try and get some grip on 'Russia' on that bigger scale. Of course, the claims on the other side about it all being 'protection' of citizens, or ethnic Russians, etc., etc., is all a little too simple too, but in the end a large part of it was a protection of a combination of interests, both then and potentially for the future, against losses being felt in an encroaching, insidious form. There are real contexts within which that exists; mainly the issue of NATO expansion, but other geopolitical and economic reconfigurations happening around that. In the end there's a kind of fluid and blurred collision of interests and of the motivations in that mix that we'd like to see as distinct, but which are not. It's never as much in such dark and distinguishable and few shades of grey as 'one side' 'wanting' this and 'the other side' 'wanting' that, etc., etc. Those same ultranationlists would love to see the whole 'Novorossiya' thing realised, but I'm pretty sure the main body of those in power would never want to take things so far, and it is ultimately an issue of protecting interests, in the same sense of both from direct present threats and from more subtly, insidiously, encroaching potential undermining of Russia's position. What is signified by the language, and references to history, is often sublter too. 'Novorossiya' is a historical term, and credit is due here for not claiming (as many do), that this is a new term created to tag onto the area of conflict in the present situation. Putin's mentioning of it does not need to be taken as some kind of territorial claim or even a 'why not maybe' have it as a part of Russia instead; it's mainly a reference to the amorphous and nuanced nature of any of those proposed territorial identities and of the lack of easy clarity in their distinctions, borders, affiliations, etc., which then demand that we see the interests and desires of the people on its territories, as well as the relationship of Russia to those areas, in a less 'you have no place here because it's called Ukraine' way. It was all the Russian empire, basically ruled from Moscow, and the distinctions didn't matter so much, for a long time. The same was also true in the Soviet Union to a large extent, and this only ended 30 years ago. And this mainly points at the reality of those places. I.e. the fact of majority Russian populations, and their natural affinity with Russia, and their natural discomfort at suddenly finding themselves in the Ukraine, when all of their lives and of the lives of most of their known ancestors they were on that same territory, and, geographically and culturally speaking, regardless of administrative borders, which just were so much less meaningful then, as mixed up with Russian culture and interests at least as much so, and probably more so, than with Ukrainian ones, and from that then, of course, their inevitable discomfort with regimes coming along that pass a law banning their language in any real official role from the offset, and regimes that did visibly benefit enormously from foreign support, and regimes that did come with some inclusion of some quite extreme anti-Russian elements; e.g. 'Praviy Sektor' or those who murdered a bunch of Russian anti-Maidan activists in Odessa. And there's a large list of misdeeds and mistakes that can be found in there if you keep looking. The point in the end is to say that the lives of the people on the ground are not just either Ukrainian or Russian, but to say that those very distinctions are a lot more gentle, nuanced and blurred than most national or ethnic distinctions we generally could find in our own parts of the world. The mention of the people's being in a sense 'one' people, is often interpreted as Putin saying Ukrainians are just Russians and that that justifies some supposed intent he has to make them his subjects, but it's also more likely about that simple reality and how it works both ways. The original 'Rus' had its capital in Kiev. No one part of that larger area was any more the 'ruling ethnicity' than any other, it's just they were very, very close and have diverged linguistically and to some extent culturally a lot more recently than other peoples in other parts of the world (and because of that also less so so far). It's not necessarily saying 'they are rightfully ours' but maybe just 'we are naturally not so separate and the issues are not so clear cut'. Crimea was actually only really given to the Ukraine in 1954, and, in fact, not by any referendum, by no choice of the population, without consulting them, by a government that is now gone and that we see as illegitimate, and one that actually didn't follow its own laws to carry out that transfer, so that that ended up a fraught issue is inevitable, and even then Crimea was still ruled chiefly from Moscow (so at the time it was a practical, geographically logical, administrative thing really, not a national divide in reality, not really something significant). Basically any region where people felt mainly Russian was equally connected to Russia going back generations and until as recently as 1991. The fact that there are those people there, and that it's not so black and white, is something the west also needs to be a lot more honest about, if it wishes to find any genuine solution. It's not like Russians just moved into some territory of a neighbouring country to try to force it to become a part of their own, but it's more like they engaged in what was going on where their people already were, and had organically been and/or slowly drifted and merged over generations, and where those people often felt themselves to be more Russian than anything else, and where, rightly or wrongly, they also felt under threat by the growing imposition of a Ukrainian homogeneity on the society they were living in, and on this territory that had never been just Ukrainian, and also where Russian strategic interests, which were not just those of the Ukraine, were involved too. That there are nefarious elements in Russia overblowing and exploiting this makes it no less true. A way needs to be found to let Crimeans have an internationally recognised expression of their own desire, and, if they choose Russia, then that and the reality of their historical Russianness need to be accepted, and, ultimately, if it went the same way, maybe the same has to be said about the two autonomous Eastern Ukrainian republics too, but, of course, better if it isn't necessary, better if the correct autonomy of the local populations is properly respected, both from Ukrainian central power, and from Russia, which also shouldn't impose its view on that population. And, of course, the rights of the whole diversity of that population need to be considered and balanced fairly. Maybe Russia might invade those areas if it feels they're threatened, but it still could conceivably refrain from ever doing so if Ukraine gives those areas enough power to decide their own direction, and to protect whatever identity and maybe mixed affiliations they feel are their own. And if the proper conversations are had about all the difficult nuances and contradictions of it all. But, yeah, if things keep being so tricky there, then maybe they would move in to absorb those two regions with their majority Russian populations, but I doubt they'd go any further. The whole issue here is mainly that too many people on all sides insist on seeing it as more simplistic than it is. All sides need to see that the other sides have legitimate interests they are trying to protect, even if all sides also have negative elements within their ranks (as is always the case) that are trying to get too much and doing things unreasonably. What tends to happen is that a caricature is made of the opposing position in order to tar it with the same brush as the least admirable minority of its members, and then antagonism and unreality grow and grow and grow. Letting that just continue is the biggest mistake, so some pride needs to be swallowed, some real close scrutiny of the real situation done, some real balanced compromises made.

comments.author: @DEVAEGIR
comments.text:
It is ludicrous to think that the Russians will invade Ukraine. Putin just scored a major victory over the US on the gas front. Events in Europe favour Russian energy policies, with the new Chancellor being moderate (essentially Merkel redoux). Why would he risk these victories and unite any enemy, current and potential? Doing this would require first getting so close to the Chinese as to become blood brothers (which will never happen). There's more. Ukraine is in the depths of economic and social crisis; annexing it, partly or wholly, would mean that Russia (quite apart from becoming the target for everyone) will need to pump billions into Ukraine for it to give any value. And finally, the Russians were invested in the fate of Crimea, a place (a) Russian to its core, demographically and culturally, and (b) of major historical and cultural significance for the average Russian. Urkaine does not share these traits. So, why would Putin, a mediocre domestic politician, but a major foreign relations player, destroy any current and potential advantages for nothing, for a place which is hostile, costly and without any benefit diplomatically, militarily, and so on?

On the other hand, you may ask, why does the US and parts of 'the West' spreading such information? (Mind you, we haven't seen a shred of proof, with the various articles about Russian movements (a) sometimes placing these movements in areas which actually are far removed from the border, and (b) lokking at only on single, a priori pushed perspective.) Money. Unipolarity is out of the window, has been for a long time, and the West's actual security concerns have for some years now relocated to the Far East. With the rise of China, NATO is quickly becoming irrelevant, so a threat very close to it must be found. Russia is convenient, because most people don't know it well or understand it (on this everyone agrees, from academics like Cohen, Mearsheimer, and Sakwa, to the most hard-line neocons in Washington); it is a traditional enemy, its government is undemocratic, it is a rising military power, and it has been outplaying the US lately (Syria, Ukraine, Nordstream; and on the diplomatic from with China, India, partly the Middle East, and in Europe). NATO needs to be relevant, NATO is a tool of US global hegemony, the hegemony is slipping away, NATO needs to remain in place for the Americans. And it is playing out.

The more annoying thing is that security specialists, as well as most politicians, know all this full well, and in many ways this is run-of-the-mill stuff. But both sides are using this as an informational axis to exploit an easily manipulated mass perception, with individuals hit relentlessly by whatever the press is hitting them with, without any opportunity to think straight.

comments.text:
What's this all about ?

From a Western European perspective...it's all about Putin trying to wind back the clock back against Gorbachov's policies, ie regain former Soviet territories. Where next? Poland God forbid.

It's also a wonder why Putin hasn't already sent Gorbachov to a Gulag prison just like he did with Nvalny et al. 

Bottom line, Putin now feels threatened because the West are now sat on his doormat, and he fears that more than anything else. 

Any potential threat in Putin's eyes to his time in power and control of Russia as a self professed lifetime leader has raised his 'Hackles'.

Putin knows full well that he is playing with fire and Brinkmanship, he is testing the waters. 

Will he succeed? that all depends on the reaction from Europe and the USA which up until now has been very weak I might add. 

The USA needs  a President Kennedy in charge and not a meek actor like Biden. 

I feel very sorry for the people of Ukraine in that when it comes down to a placing of the chips, the USA stands aside as will the pathetic bunch of politicians from Brussels. 

Ukraine  should not be bullied by a Dictator like Putin, Ukraine has a legitimate right to be a Sovereign nation just like Russia has.

Let's hope Ukraine have a few Aces up their sleeve to finaly bloody the nose of Putin, and make him think that some nations will not be bullied into submission.

comments.author: @jasmonate
comments.text: I think the best approach to this situation, glazing over a bunch of political posturing that makes this difficult, is a series of five-party observed referendums. Ukraine, the U.S., Russia, each independence-seeking krai, and a fifth neutral nation (like Switzerland or Mongolia) observe three referendums, one each for Crimea, Donetsk, and Luhansk. This oversight should stop any little green men from interfering, though I think at least Crimea and Donetsk will vote to leave Ukraine. If this is the case, and it’s verified to be a fair election, let them leave. I think they will all vote to leave, removing much of the contention and all of the hot conflict from the negotiation. After that, proceed with getting Russia to recognize the border of Ukraine as final unless another krai holds a referendum within Ukraine. Bind Ukraine to honor legitimate referendums as a condition of them gaining access to the European community. A lot easier to say than to do, but it’s better than parking tanks across the border and waiting for someone to flinch.

comments.author: @ERPRocks
comments.text:
Our reaction as a nation to the events between Russia and the Ukraine over the last seem to have more to do with our traditional cold war views on Russian and less to do with which country was more in the right. Start with the fact that the crisis began with the overthrow of the democratically elected Ukrainian government that immediately began a campaign of persecution of the ethnic Russian population, who consequently rose in a counter revolution.

Either Ukraine sorts out its corruption problems and becomes a modern European state … or it becomes more allied with Russia.   Equating corruption with a Russian association and clean government with a European orientation. That Ukraine was torn between a clean European relationship and a dirty Russian one.

The description of Russia’s annexation of Crimea as illegal is unreasonably simplistic. It ignores not just recent and long-term history of the region but also the cultures of the people who live in the region The only reason that Crimea was part of the Ukraine was because of an administrative change made by Khrushchev in 1954 a distinction which had no real impact under a single Soviet Union but became highly problematic after the breakup and not something that any knowledgeable person expected to continue.

The fact is that for most of modern history, Crimea was a part of Russia. The people who lived there spoke Russian and considered themselves Russian. In Russa, a notoriously frozen nation, Crimea was Russa’s traditional warm vacation spot. Strategically, Crimea was the traditional home of the Russian Black Sea fleet, something that did not change even after the breakup of the Soviet Union. A little research would tell you that upon the break-up of the Soviet Union many experts publicly voiced the question of what would happen to Crimea, with the consensus at the time being that this issue would have to deferred to the future once the chaos of the Soviet breakup was resolved.

Your descriptions of the Euromaidan protests is both biased and simplistic. It the sharp divide that existed between the ethnic Russia located mostly in the more prosperous south and east and the ethnic Ukrainians located primarily in the western and northern part of the country. More importantly, Viktor Yanukovych, is an ethnic Russian and elected under as part of a Russian leaning party in democratic election that was widely considered fair. While he was in power, most experts believed that the Ukraine would remain in the Russian sphere of influence. The revolt in Kiev was an expression of the local ethnic Ukrainians and not that of the majority Ukrainian population. From Russia’s perspective the ethnic Ukrainians threw out the democratically elected population. Furthermore, by turning to the west for support, they brought the issue of Crimea to a head. Anyone familiar with the situation could have predicted what would happen; that they would annex Crimea and actively support the ethnic Russians living on their border. In their mind this was not an illegal act but just a re-establishment of traditional historical borders.

comments.author: @igoralekseyev3347
comments.text:
In my opinion, the West's policy towards the Ukraine-Russia conflict has been the worst of both worlds.

1) By encouraging Ukraine to join NATO and the EU, they have aggravated Russia, which sees both of these outcomes as a fundamental threat to its own security. I personally don't believe that NATO or the EU plans to attack Russia, but that's beside the point - the fact is that the Russian leadership sees it as such, and that's what matters.

Russia reacted to this by escalating the conflict in Ukraine, annexing Crimea and actively supporting pro-Russian forces in the East. From the Russian perspective, this is a perfectly rational reaction. Whatever the morality of it, it makes perfect sense from the Russian perspective.

It is *essential* to understand that Russia views the Ukrainian question as *absolutely vital* to its national security. This isn't megalomania or Soviet nostalgia. This is  the Cuban missile crisis, when the Americans absolutely lost their shit when they learned that their main geopolitical rival was about to set up a military base right on their doorstep.

Understanding this reality is essential, because it means that no matter what the West does, Russia will not back down. They will even go to war - because the way they see it, if Ukraine joins the EU or NATO, they've already lost without a fight. Geopolitically speaking, Ukraine joining NATO/EU puts the West one move away from putting Russia in 'checkmate'.

2) The West also did nothing to protect Ukraine. Even now, it's clear that in the event of an all-out war no-one will come to Ukraine's aid, and the Ukrainians will be alone against Russia. Whatever the outcome of that war, it's clear that it will utterly devastate Ukraine, which will be on the defensive and thus the main battleground in an all-out, modern war.

Essentially, the West has set the Ukrainians up for disaster. They've encouraged Ukraine to provoke Russia and have done virtually nothing to support them. It's the geopolitical equivalent of telling someone to attack a bear with a fork, and giving them a thumbs up and telling them 'you've got this!'

3) In a perfect world, Ukraine should be able to choose its own foreign policy alignment and everyone would respect it. But it's not a perfect world, and the reality is that actions have consequences, which are not fair or favourable.

comments.author: @BuckeyeRutabaga
comments.text:
I say not worth it.  Even though Ukraine may then end up tilting more toward Eurasia, I don't think you presented any argument to support the speculation that Russia will eventually occupy Ukraine.  I don't see any motive for Russia to occupy Ukraine.  I can see a motive to support the eastern Ukrainian breakaway regions but any further than that... I doubt it.  Too expensive and unnecessary.  I think there's more evidence to support that Russia would rather see Ukraine independent yet friendly to Russia and without any NATO presence - to serve as a buffer zone of sorts. 

It's worth mentioning the practical implications behind the EU Association agreement that Yanukovych decided not to sign in 2013.  Russia was also opposing the Ukraine EU Association because at the time, the Ukraine-Russia border was very hollow and once the Ukrainian markets opened up to Western European products, they would flood the Russian market through the hollow border and without any customs duties paid.  Russia under Putin has been very protectionist about their domestic producers and could not guarantee domestic market protection against illegally imported EU products through the Ukrainian-Russian border.  Even Belarus - staunch Russia's ally, has been repeatedly scorned by Russia for not keeping their EU border sealed sufficiently from EU products contraband syphoning onto Russia's markets. If Russian government wasn't worried about the Ukraine-EU Association because of that alone then that would mean that the Russian government wasn't doing its job.

comments.author: @Aepigon
comments.text:
I can't stress enough how misleading this video is, comparing the Russian economy to the Italian one, via presentation of their respective GDP in nominal values. 
Russian nominal GDP, just just shows how bad the relationship between the Russian Ruble and the American Dollar is and how undervalued it is - thanks to the western sanctions, but also to the Russians themselves, who deliberately lower it even further. Lower exchange rate means that their exports sell easily, bringing external currencies back to Russia. What this means is that the value of an item in Russia is much lower than the value of an item in USA - for one buck you can buy much more in Russia than in the states,  but what one fails to realize is the scope of industrial production in Russia. 
Currently in terms of production and purchasing power, which by it self is the real way to show the power of an economy, the  Russian economy is only slightly lower that the German one, bringing it on 6th place in the world. Check GDP (PPP) on the net, and after that you may quit dreaming that Russia is weak based on the false assumption, this poor video presented here. (Huge dislike from me)

a. Europe is already switching away from using gas as an energy source (through mostly renewables, but let's not forget that it can stop closing and also reopen nuclear reactors to get more energy)
b. it can get what it strictly needs from other countries (for example, the price of gas in the US is x10 lower than in Europe)
c. if Russia forces Europe to switch away from gas and Europe does so successfully, then Russia will have to sell their gas at much lower prices (as a lot of its previous buyers now don't buy gas at all), thus debilitating the Russian economy and reducing its geopolitical power. It might sound weird, but fixing the energy crisis in Europe (through the embracement of nuclear, renewables and energy storing systems such as hydro) is a way to fight against Russia.

comments.author: @seamusoneill99
comments.text:
Russia can shut off much of Europe's gas supply whenever they want. It would cripple the Russian economy but it would also immobilize Northern/Eastern Europe for long enough that the Russians could force them to give in on Ukraine, since I doubt European public opinion would tolerate having no heat in the winter (i.e. their own personal hardship) just to defend Ukraine. In the end this might hurt Russia more than Europe but it seems that on average Russians care more about national honor than economic prosperity, at least on this issue and at least in the short term. Russians are also more used to economic/material deprivation than most European nations are, so they could withstand an economic collapse for longer.

The most stable feasible option from a geostrategic standpoint would be for NATO's leadership and Putin to sit down and hammer out a compromise whereby Ukraine would legally cede Russia control of Crimea, Donetsk, and Luhansk, and then NATO make it very clear that any further advances by Russia against Ukrainian territory would be met with military force, i.e. draw a "red line in the sand" and then actually stick to it by placing large numbers of NATO troops in Ukraine between the Dneiper and the new Russian border. Of course, the main issue with this is that it's not 1885 anymore, and rival powers actually have to take public opinion into account and not openly divide up territory like the imperialists they still often are. Ukrainians probably wouldn't stand for the territorial concessions and wouldn't trust NATO for a generation if they acquiesced, unless NATO actually followed through and built up a military presence in Eastern Ukraine to forcefully block any further Russian advances. This is a problem that the EU can't solve since it isn't a military alliance.

comments.author: @moonlighting9782
comments.text:
So many foundational assumptions that form the basis of what's said in this video aren't correct. Way too much credit given to Ukraines ability to conduct large offensive operations when in fact they have hugely depleted manpower, are low on munitions, artillery and don't take into account the revised strategy of the Russians. 
No more will the Russians make allowances to minimise military casualties. Kherson civilians were evacuated as it meant delicate offensives in the area so now the Russian gloves are off. They have much more manpower, are bringing in better hardware and no longer have the opinion that the West/Ukraine will negotiate in good faith. Trust has been lost and the words of the west can't be relied on. 
As for the decreased use of drones it could well be that there is little need of minor offensives in light of knowing there will be huge offensives to come, better to reserve the drones that have already proved effective.
So many other factors, Winter coming which is when the Russians historically have proved effective in battle. Also the enthusiasm among the Europeans is varied and by and large waning except for the UK government. For those that actually pay attention to Putin's rhetoric will know that Kherson hasn't been relinquished, Russia will not concede 'sovereign' territory. It's a big no no fo the Russians so at some point in this war you can expect attempts to retake it. Big geopolitical moves happening elsewhere that may well affect the situation. The rest of the world is feeling much more free to voice discontent and and there are many stands being taken from outright rhetoric in the UN to informal off the cuff conversations. Xi and Trudeau being an example. Xi wasn't shy in voicing displeasure and all this serves to weaken the West.
There are too many nuances, factors and variables moving around to convey everything in a post but the wind is blowing in the right direction for Eurasia, the East and the middle East. De dollarization, new trade blocs (BRICS, SCO) and Chinas BRI along with a diminished Europe all come into the equation. Western leaders are looking inwards at domestic issues and it's only the US, Poland and UK that are eager for continuing the madness. So much could happen but Russia losing in Ukraine isn't a part of it.

comments.author: @Scorpio.1989
comments.text:
*CAN* Ukraine retake Crimea? Yes.

*SHOULD* Ukraine retake Crimea? No, at least not yet.



Ukraine's next offensive should be down south to retake *Melitopol*  *Berdiansk* and *Mariupol* along with most of not all of the *Zaporizhzhia Oblast* cutting Crimea off from resupply…

Retaking the southern coast would threaten the Russian Black Sea Fleet which is largely sitting idle in Crimea, it'll largely cut off military supply lines to the Kherson oblast, Ukraine already have partisans doing some good work in this region and it gives Ukraine a way to out flank the separatist's WWI style trenches further North…

Retaking the majority of the Zaporizhzhia Oblast and keeping the Kerch bridge (Crimean Bridge) out of commission would seriously threaten Russia’s hold on Crimea (internationally recognized Ukrainian territory). If Ukraine 🇺🇦 can retake the Zaporizhzhia Oblast and maybe even force the Russians back to pre war lines in South Eastern Ukraine, Russia 🇷🇺 could be forced to the negotiating table and accept Ukrainian terms for a total withdrawal from all internationally recognized Ukrainian territory…

Russia has demonstrated that they can’t hold Ukrainian territory against a sustained against a well planned attack that is properly supported and supplied, all Ukraine 🇺🇦 needs is the training, tools, and the nessary logistics to do the job, it’s clear that they have the will power, but they need better tanks to take on Russian armor (early models of the M1A1 Abrams or Leopard tanks would be fantastic options) they can also use some air support, we have a couple of brand new AH-6 Little Birds that went straight to the boneyard from the factory because they were ordered by Afghanistan…

The winter time would be best utilized if Ukraine could rotate front line units to Kiev or Lviv for more training and some rest, and hit Russian supply lines putting a further strain on the already strained Russian logistics (continued attacks on the Kerch bridge (Crimean Bridge))would definitely hurt Russian logistics, so Ukraine could launch the new offensive as soon as spring hits…

Lot of bull💩.

U.S. inventory isn't running low.
In U.S. circles, if you have 50,000 GMLRS rockets and you give Ukraine 5,000 or 10%, that's a flag to increase production.
The U.S. has actually increased its GMLRS inventory since Feb 24th. It's also increased its inventory of HIMARS rocket launchers.

There's only certain munitions that have declined in the U.S. inventory and some of that needed to be replaced because of aging. They've began shipping the HAWK anti air missiles of which they have a lot, but they're no longer used and have been replaced by newer tech. Thus, those HAWK missile were destined for destruction.

Bottom line. The U.S. has determined that its inventory needs to be increased, not because they are running low, but because of what they've seen in Ukraine says the inventory should have been higher to start with. That and concern about China/Taiwan situation.

comments.author: @konstanzeallsopp3087
comments.text:
Are you ALL really equally dim??? And trust me, I have had to write this commentary to 14 other video channels. The Dnipro (or Dnieper) river starts its journey from north to south rising in the Valdai Hills near Smolensk, Russia, before flowing through Belarus and Ukraine to the Black Sea. When it reaches Ukraine it initially flows south and then turns southeast before making a very sharp 100-degree angle to the west, at the town of Zaporizhia and there, spreading into a large man-made reservoir for freshwater with the Dnieper Hydroelectric Station and the Dneprostroi Dam lying approximately equidistant between Zaporizhia and Kerson, the Dnipro continues its journey west-south-west and eventually into the Black Sea.
So in Kherson, there is no east or west side of the river, OK, just a north and south side. At the moment in and around Kherson, the Ukrainian soldiers and liberated Ukrainian civilians are on the NORTH bank of the Dnipro, if they are lucky, and the Russians are on the SOUTH bank, if THEY are lucky, and might be making their way even further south as fast as they can. Got the geographics? Don't just repeat other people's stupid words or statements. Do your own research and come down on the clever side. It is VERY easy to see on Google Maps that the river flows east to west at Kherson. Hope that helps in the future.
Слава Україні

comments.author: @studiotwilldee
comments.text:
The domestic environment Putin has fostered in Russia makes any kind of settlement impossible.

1) The ultranationalists will absolutely eviscerate Putin if he cedes Russian territory.

2) Putin has annexed most of Eastern Ukraine into Russia.

3) He does not control all of this territory and does not have the military capabilities to seize it.

This means that any "peace" he agrees to is simply an interlude to a new invasion. Ukraine has momentum and the west is finding out that Russia has a great deal less leverage than they assumed.

The negotiated-peace faction is willing to completely jeopardize long term regional stability for a short term pause on the bloodshed.

Russia will continue to be a threat until its military is shattered. Ukraine has the momentum to achieve this so long as we don't blink when Putin acts from desperation.
