comments.text:
Crimea. Ugh! What a mess. It was basically a built in booby trap although it wasn't the original intent by Krushchev. It was a largely autonomous region within Ukraine prior to 2014, so that concept failed. What then if Ukraine succeeds in retaking Crimea? It'll have a resentful, antagonistic region to deal with all over again. I don't envy the people who will be tasked with trying to figure out how to pacify the population that supported Putin's actions, let alone figure out a way that everyone can coexist peacefully there.

When Russia offered Kaliningrad to Poland or Lithuania, they both wisely declined. The governments had the foresight to see what a problem having a significant Russian minority would have been. Pity Ukraine hadn't done the same.

comments.author: @milutzuk
comments.text: I think it would be a bad decision from a strategic point of view to conclude the war at the current borders. It could be a good political decision for the West, but politics of a democratic country is usually short-sighted and doesn't always align with long-term strategic interests. By losing Donbas and Crimea Ukraine loses a good chunk of its economic output and it doesn't get a guarantee that Russia would not try again some stunt. Donbas and Crimea can be used by Ukraine to obtain Western investments, which would guarantee a faster rebuild and Russia would get a further reduction of its military power and political stability. With that dam half in the hands of the Russians, Kherson would still be threatened and it would be a much longer common border to be defended. On the other hand, I doubt the West doesn't look at the war expenses and doesn't draw some conclusions, like how about much cheaper intelligent ammunition (and this is a whole discussion) so they are probably already looking into alternatives so the US declarations that their ammunition stores are depleted doesn't fly too far. Finally, no matter where the borders would be, Ukraine would probably maintain some form of military preparedness and integration with NATO, again, in case Russia... The best outcome for everybody is to restore the pre-2014 borders and have Russia pay for Ukraine's reconstruction but that would only happen if a regime more focused on business and less on nationalism takes the power in Russia.

24 Jesus left the temple and was walking away when his disciples came up to him to call his attention to its buildings. 2 “Do you see all these things?” he asked. “Truly I tell you, not one stone here will be left on another; every one will be thrown down.”

3 As Jesus was sitting on the Mount of Olives, the disciples came to him privately. “Tell us,” they said, “when will this happen, and what will be the sign of your coming and of the end of the age?”

4 Jesus answered: “Watch out that no one deceives you. 5 For many will come in my name, claiming, ‘I am the Messiah,’ and will deceive many. 6 You will hear of wars and rumors of wars, but see to it that you are not alarmed. Such things must happen, but the end is still to come. 7 Nation will rise against nation, and kingdom against kingdom. There will be famines and earthquakes in various places. 8 All these are the beginning of birth pains.

9 “Then you will be handed over to be persecuted and put to death, and you will be hated by all nations because of me. 10 At that time many will turn away from the faith and will betray and hate each other, 11 and many false prophets will appear and deceive many people. 12 Because of the increase of wickedness, the love of most will grow cold, 13 but the one who stands firm to the end will be saved. 14 And this gospel of the kingdom will be preached in the whole world as a testimony to all nations, and then the end will come.

15 “So when you see standing in the holy place ‘the abomination that causes desolation,’[a] spoken of through the prophet Daniel—let the reader understand— 16 then let those who are in Judea flee to the mountains. 17 Let no one on the housetop go down to take anything out of the house. 18 Let no one in the field go back to get their cloak. 19 How dreadful it will be in those days for pregnant women and nursing mothers! 20 Pray that your flight will not take place in winter or on the Sabbath. 21 For then there will be great distress, unequaled from the beginning of the world until now—and never to be equaled again.

22 “If those days had not been cut short, no one would survive, but for the sake of the elect those days will be shortened. 23 At that time if anyone says to you, ‘Look, here is the Messiah!’ or, ‘There he is!’ do not believe it. 24 For false messiahs and false prophets will appear and perform great signs and wonders to deceive, if possible, even the elect. 25 See, I have told you ahead of time.

26 “So if anyone tells you, ‘There he is, out in the wilderness,’ do not go out; or, ‘Here he is, in the inner rooms,’ do not believe it. 27 For as lightning that comes from the east is visible even in the west, so will be the coming of the Son of Man. 28 Wherever there is a carcass, there the vultures will gather.

29 “Immediately after the distress of those days

“‘the sun will be darkened,
    and the moon will not give its light;
the stars will fall from the sky,
    and the heavenly bodies will be shaken.’[b]
30 “Then will appear the sign of the Son of Man in heaven. And then all the peoples of the earth[c] will mourn when they see the Son of Man coming on the clouds of heaven, with power and great glory.[d] 31 And he will send his angels with a loud trumpet call, and they will gather his elect from the four winds, from one end of the heavens to the other.

32 “Now learn this lesson from the fig tree: As soon as its twigs get tender and its leaves come out, you know that summer is near. 33 Even so, when you see all these things, you know that it[e] is near, right at the door. 34 Truly I tell you, this generation will certainly not pass away until all these things have happened. 35 Heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will never pass away.

The Day and Hour Unknown

36 “But about that day or hour no one knows, not even the angels in heaven, nor the Son,[f] but only the Father. 37 As it was in the days of Noah, so it will be at the coming of the Son of Man. 38 For in the days before the flood, people were eating and drinking, marrying and giving in marriage, up to the day Noah entered the ark; 39 and they knew nothing about what would happen until the flood came and took them all away. That is how it will be at the coming of the Son of Man. 40 Two men will be in the field; one will be taken and the other left. 41 Two women will be grinding with a hand mill; one will be taken and the other left.

42 “Therefore keep watch, because you do not know on what day your Lord will come. 43 But understand this: If the owner of the house had known at what time of night the thief was coming, he would have kept watch and would not have let his house be broken into. 44 So you also must be ready, because the Son of Man will come at an hour when you do not expect him.l

comments.author: @Vivian-fq8yf
comments.text:
Russia’s invasion is not stalling at all, what so many people in the West are currently failing to understand is that the Russians never intended to capture Kiev. It doesn’t make any sense as there were less Russian soldiers around Kiev than Ukrainian soldiers. Generally in war the rule is that offensive force needs a 3:1 ratio advantage against the defender to take a city. What is more likely is that there was a feint, a deception, which was used in order to fixate the Ukrainian military away from the Donbass region. At the beginning the Russian objectives were to destroy Ukraine’s capacity to mobilise, and that was successful. Nearly all of Ukraine’s fuel depots have been destroyed with Russian missile strikes. 

This gives Russia room to focus on the Donbass, which the Russians stated from the beginning was their main focus, on protecting the two republics in the east. The reason this isn’t going as fast as some in the West predicted is because it’s more about grinding down the Ukrainian army than taking more territory at a time. The Ukrainians continue to launch counteroffensives on places like Kherson and in the Donbass, and each time they do they fail and suffer heavy casualties. It’s much easier to defend than it is to launch a new offensive immediately, and because of this the Russian casualties have been reduced dramatically since the start of the operation. Ukraine has the second largest army in Europe, over 600,000 soldiers if you count reserves, compared to Russia’s 200,000. For the Russians it’s just a matter of destroying and grinding down the Ukrainian army before they decide to make major advances again in the Donbass, and eventually Nikolaev and Odessa as well. This is a very soft invasion, the Russians haven’t used any of their modern weaponry. In the beginning they didn’t even really use their Air Force. They have bombers they could use to devastate Ukraine, but they haven’t done so, because they want to preserve the existing infrastructure and civilian population within the country. Civilians are dying at a much lower rate in Ukraine than during the Iraq War. 

I get that this explanation is much less dramatic and exciting for Western audiences, because they are being told that this is an all out war on television, but this is just not how it is in reality.

comments.author: @richieboy1971
comments.text:
The interpretation of this ground war in Ukraine in the Western MSM has been badly miss read and ultimately, this will come back to haunt them when the real truth starts to emerge. 
Many in the West still believe that Russia wanted to take the whole of Ukraine in this conflict, and that simply was never the case. 
Russia stated its goals early doors and only ever wanted to achieve the de-militarisation and de-nazification of Eastern Ukraine. Eight years of civil war in the Donbass region since 2014, had led to around 15,000 lives being lost, and these mainly comprised of cultural Russians living in Eastern Ukraine. 
Russia put a fairly big force of reserves into the area around the north west of Kyiv, which was a tactical move to draw Ukrainian troops quickly up from the South and the East to protect Kyiv. 
Those troops mainly sat still for weeks and didn’t ever threaten Kyiv much apart from making it look like they were ready to attack Kyiv. 
No army wants to storm a city, as that takes up tens of thousands of troops and is simply not practical. So it’s easier to try and surround cities and restrict movement, supplies etc. 
So with the South of Ukraine now weaker due to the withdrawal of troops back to defend Kyiv, Russian troops piled into the south and took ground relatively quickly, but not methodically. 
Heavier losses were taken by Russia in the early parts of this conflict as they often moved quickly to take ground without clearing out the areas behind them, and often being caught out from pockets of Ukrainian troops. 
Phase two of this conflict is a lot different as Russia are methodically taking out every bit of resistance in a much slower and controlled fashion using heavy artillery to decimate Ukrainian positions first before advancing forward. We saw this tactic used by NATO forces in the gulf war in 1991, when Nato forces spent two months carrying out extensive air sorties to cripple any Iraq positions before committing any troops on the ground in Iraq. 
This sadly led to many tens of thousands of civilian deaths as military equipment was strategically placed by the Iraq army in key residential areas. 
So many pundits are now thinking that the Russian campaign is stalling, but don’t realise that it’s simply a tactical change. 
Whatever happens in Ukraine, it is not looking good, and sadly many more lives will be lost. 
The vast size of Ukraine is also a major factor and this is not going to be a quick phase of this war. 
So I wouldn’t write Russia off yet as it does have by far, the upper hand and the last few weeks have seen some tactical missile strikes designed to cripple the railway system that is being used to transport the flood of military hardware being sent in from the West from reaching the front lines. 
But with Ukraine refusing any sort of peace talks, I fear that this is going to end up costing thousands of lives in a war, which Ukraine alone cannot win, and NATO cannot join without triggering WWIII.

comments.text:
General mobilisation? I suspect not. It would only reinforce FAILURE. Bearing in mind the Russian military have been unable to provide adequate logistical support to their forces already in Ukraine, adding more troops on the ground would just collapse their struggling logistics system. Furthermore, adding a large number of hastily trained (I'm being kind) conscripts, many of whom will have little enthusiasm for the war, will not alter the strategic situation for the Russian military. In addition, full mobilisation will just increase pressure on the already crippled Russian economy. 

Putin has already lost this conflict. Russia has failed to achieve it's key strategic objectives. Casualties have been high in both men and materiel. It's estimated by British intelligence that the Russian army is losing the equivalent of a battalion sized battle group every single day of the conflict. Russia cannot sustain that casualty rate. Meanwhile, the Ukrainian's have mobilised most of the able bodied male populace- so they possess more man power than Russia. More importantly, the Ukrainian defenders are highly motivated with good morale- unlike the Russia army, which has been mauled badly. 

I suspect that once the newly supplied heavy weapons from the US, UK and Germany reach the frontline, the Ukrainians will launch a powerful counter-attack. 

The Russian military is now a complete laughing stock in military circles. Their logistic support is poor. Their command and control and calibre of junior and middle rank leadership is abysmal. Their military doctrine is obsolete- entrenched in the Cold War, relying on large, slow moving armoured formations. Much of their equipment is poorly maintained and unreliable- as it was during the Cold War. Their tactical awareness is poor, their troops are poorly trained, and ill-disciplined in many instances. They seem incapable of conducting combined arms operations effectively. Just compare the US and UK military with that of Russia, and they are leagues ahead in just about every key area- especially in the calibre of their personnel and leadership. All the Russian's are fit for is throwing lots of shells into civilian areas.

Russia is nothing more than a Paper Tiger, a regional European power, certainly not a superpower in conventional terms. The US military would go through the Russian forces like a hot knife through butter. It's ironic to think that at one time, the Russian military was feared during the Cold War, now they are a laughing stock. All those years NATO over estimated the capability of the Russian's and the Warsaw Pact.

comments.author: @markhughes7927
comments.text:
3:10
Struggled in Kiev? It encircled the city in the early stages until it became evident that no negotiations were intended/permitted from the Kiev government. It then redeployed to more useful work in the eastern region. Initial reporting left no damaged building unphotographed to give example of Russian barbarity yet these days ‘statesmen’ from over the world convene in the beautiful city there. The example of Mariupol disabused the world of the ‘noble conduct’ of Ukrainian forces - the work of independent journalists exposed the population there’s’ curse upon these forces - the damage they did and the crimes committed. The Ukrainian army is mostly unsupplied and now reliant on conscripts young, old and inexperienced both. Humanitarian relief blows in with the Russian forces to the openly filmed joy of inhabitants and the grateful display of red white and blue bunting. Meanwhile captured British mercenaries y/t tell of the murder by Azov of colleagues and other foreigners as they have no international status, protection, or identity and so are easy prey for people for whom murder and robbery is a simple way to acquire pocket money. The de-nazification of Europe by Britain the U.S. and Russia in the early 1940s was a triumph of the Allies over Axis powers. Unfortunately the job is now left to Russia alone and her former allies have ‘crossed the floor’ so to speak and joined forces with a military culture which worked with Central European military of the 30s and 40s. To say this requires knowledge of the war which has raged for 8 years now and of which this is a part - but there is plenty out there which may provide a school for this knowledge. The rest is pretty much hollow rhetoric, junk armaments at ‘lend-lease’ cost = debt-trap from the nether lockers of NATO forces - a lot of it yet beyond the capability of the remnant of the Ukr. forces to manage: these will be updated in those donor countries and the armament business in Britain and the U.S. will be brisk while Ukraine will be left to bear the long term cost for the most part though not wholly useless equipment. The first essential idea to get clear in the head about this war is that it is NOT about Ukraine - it is about confronting Russia and poisoning the world’s mind about this remarkable, inventive, plucky and resolute country.

comments.author: @octowuss1888
comments.text: Has Russia's Invasion Stalled Again? No. They are winning hands down. They have complete air supremacy and are running about 250 sorties a day and continue to launch regular missile strikes. (They have launched over 1500 cruise missiles so far in the war!) Almost all of the arms factories in Ukraine have been destroyed, along with all of the oil refineries, munitions and oil depots, military training facilities and transport infrastructure. Ukraine has lost at least 2000 tanks. The Ukrainians are finding it hard to move troops, ammo and food around. They are limited to small, temporary, tactical victories: shoot down an aircraft, kill a tank, destroy fuel deport etc. MSM praises these as examples of how Ukraine is winning, but really they have no strategic presence and Russia controls the battlefield. On the Eastern front, Ukraine losses about 300 troops killed each day in trenches by the massive Russian artillery barrages. Ukraine is forced to withdraw the remains of their troops from those positions, whereby the Russians occupy those areas and the whole situation repeats. Slow and steady progress. Sooner or later the Ukrainian army will break and then Russia will move quickly to encircle their troops. Game over.

comments.author: @squirepraggerstope3591
comments.text:
In brief, what 'Vlad-the-Invader' seems to be trying to do is pursue much the same o/a aim but on a slightly more modest scale. The goal of outright regime change and use of all Ukraine as a client-ally / buffer state, has probably been dropped. The fallback is very likely to be an attempt to split the country, although the hope of occupying everything east of the Dnieper, plus a swathe of more westerly coastal territory including Odessa and a western border with Romania and Moldova, is likely still much too ambitious.

What will probably emerge, then, represents mainly the implicit acceptance by Vlad of a "catch 22" type conundrum created by his own annexation of Crimea and support for the Donbas separatists.

IE: Russia will end up with most of the territory of the two eastern oblasts, Donetsk and Luhansk, plus Crimea, plus a stretch of SE Ukraine (incl Mariupol) connecting the two. With a new stretch of border running roughly west from the N/S frontier west of Donetsk to the Dnieper just south of Zaporizhzhia, and then along the left bank of the river to the sea...

...leaving a just modestly reduced yet now almost wholly pro-Western Ukraine with virtually no residual pro-Russian / separatist minority. A country which still has a population of 37+ millions and substantial lands east of the Dnieper, with urban centers like Kramatorsk, Pavlohrad, Poltava, Kharkiv, Sumy and Chernihiv.

So to put it bluntly, poor Vlad will have failed.

-No successful occupation followed by the installation of  puppet regime in Kyiv.
-No chance for the forseeable future of a Ukrainian election installing a government more sympathetic to Moscow
-No chance of stirring the separatist pot anymore, as the vast majority of those who identify as Russian (in Donbas and Crimea) and want to live in Russia..   ...will already do so.

comments.author: @janjop31
comments.text:
For people disputing who violated what first, Russia annexed Crimea before the Minsk agreements were established. The annexation occurred in March 2014, following a controversial referendum held in Crimea on March 16, where the local population voted to join Russia. This move was widely condemned by Ukraine and the international community as illegal, as it clearly violated the Respect for Sovereignty and Territorial Integrity portion of the 1994 Budapest Memorandum.

The Minsk Accords, which aimed to address the ongoing conflict in Eastern Ukraine, were signed later, with the first agreement (Minsk I) in September 2014 and the second agreement (Minsk II) in February 2015. Thus, the annexation of Crimea was a significant event that preceded the Minsk agreements and set the stage for the subsequent conflict in Eastern Ukraine.

In short, Russia violated the 1994 Budapest Memorandum before the Minsk agreements were ever established. Russia's actions in Crimea demonstrate that it cannot be trusted to uphold its international commitments.

While the U.S. and UK did provide security assurances to Ukraine as part of the Budapest Memorandum, the agreement appears to have lacked robust enforcement mechanisms. This has left ambiguity around the specific obligations of these countries to protect Ukraine, even after Russia's clear violation of the memorandum's terms.

comments.text: The Russians will never stop attacking Ukraine until they have subdued it. They just want the entire Ukraine to re-become a part of Russia. NOW, back in 1990-91 when the Soviet Union collapsed, Ukraine dutifully approached the Russian Duma, their Congress, and asked for permission to withdraw from the USSR. The request was approved. Everything had been handled by the book, and Ukraine just went on with their lives, not bothering anybody. In fact, many of the USSR's ballistic missiles were located in Ukraine, and they happily gave them back to the Russkies, and in return the Russians guaranteed, in a treaty, that Ukraine would remain its own sovereign nation, with the Russians doing all the guaranteeing.  Now, Putin wants Ukraine back, because he needs all the Ukrainian men to be a part of his army. Ukraine was the biggest satellite of Russia, in terms of men that could be drafted. Putin will take everything that he can get for free, upon the false promise that he will stop the war. All he'll actually do is go back, rebuild his army and replace all the destroyed military vehicles. Then he will re-attack and try to seize them again. That's exactly what happened in 1938 when he attacked Lithuania, Stalin attacked them, but the Lithuanians, too, wiped the floor with the butts of the Russians. So Stalin signed a peace treaty, withdrew all his men, went back home and rebuilt their army and re-attached. This time they won, and the Lithuanians were under the Russian thumb for the next 50+ years. If anybody would believe that Putin will take a small portion of Ukraine and then be happy about it, Well, I've got some oceanfront property in Arizona to sell to you.

comments.text:
Awhile back I read in a British news report, when Obama was President,  that some potential British investors in Ukraines Electrical Company told Ukraine's leaders that the friend of Putin heading up their system asked for bribes before they could make an investment.  Ukraine's leaders fired the man asking for bribes and who supported reuniting with Russia.  As a political move, not an economic move, Ukraines leaders put Hunter Biden in as the Head of it's Electrical Company.  I suppose thinking an American there would discourage Putin.

I don't know, but suspect if Trump had been President at the time, the Ukraine would have offered the job to Trump's son-in-law.  Ukraine didn't care which American Party, they just wanted to send a message to Putin.  Fast forward to the Biden-Trump 2016 election.  Trump asked the Ukraine to say that Biden's son was the one who asked the British investors for bribes, not Putin's ally in the job.  The Ukraine, between a rock and a hard place,  wouldn't lie for Trump.  I doubt they cared, but they were in a tricky decision.

Knowing Trump and his love for revenge to some degree, it's for sure Trump will abandon the Ukraine in favor of Putin as payback now.  Do the Democrats ever do such a thing?  Of course.  Like the Bible says, "All have sinned and fallen short of the Glory of God."  Ukraine's leaders know they are in trouble now and will have to lean on their European allies as Trump will dump them for not lying for him in 2016. 
Such is the world today and as it always has been.

comments.text:
Getting Ukraine to give up 20% of its territory will be difficult.  No country wants to give up territory, and that's asking for an enormous amount of it with nothing in return.  It's not a "deal", it's straight up surrender and capitulation.  That _can_ happen (see WW2 Japan) but it's not easy. 
Getting Ukraine to agree to neutrality will be effectively impossible.  That would be the equivalent of telling Russia "feel free to attack again next year and Ukraine will have even less support because we wouldn't want to break our new neutrality deal".  Ukraine simply won't trust such a deal no matter how many promises are made.

comments.author: @FalconFastest123
comments.text: Trump's plan is simple brinkmanship. It is a strategy he mastered in his career and which he explains many times in his book Art of the Deal. The strategy is basically starting at less than half of what you want, and threatening to walk away (and meaning it) if the other side doesn't make concessions. In this case, he will play brinkmanship against Ukraine by threatening to withdraw aid and brinkmanship against Russia by threatening to increase said aid to Ukraine, which would bog them down even more. This would theoretically bring both sides to the table, and force a settlement, unless Russia thinks it can win regardless of US aid to Ukraine. Ukraine, however, knows it cannot survive without US aid, so it will negotiate. It's in its own best interest to do so. And if Russia balks and the US sends more aid to Ukraine as a result, we may see the tides begin to shift in Ukraine's favor in the war.

Then there’s the whole ‘de-nazification of Ukraine’ narrative you hear in Russian propaganda. It’s a loaded term, but there’s some context to it. Some modern Ukrainian national heroes were Nazi collaborators. One prominent example is Bandera, a Ukrainian nationalist whose actions led to the deaths of hundreds of thousands of Poles, Czechs, and Russians - many of them innocent women and children - simply because they weren’t Ukrainian. In just a few months, these numbers far exceeded the Ukrainian casualties since 2022. The point is, it’s not all black and white, as mainstream media often portrays.

comments.author: @alexandergries4769
comments.text:
This video shows a clear bias, which we in Europe increasingly recognize as Kremlin-driven narratives. A hallmark of this is the repeated claim of a so-called military stalemate. However, almost all credible military analysts in the West agree that Russia's resources are visibly depleting. The notion of a stalemate is, therefore, a deliberate falsehood aimed at shaping public perception.

To illustrate: Imagine a mayor promising his citizens great wealth if he gambles all their money at the casino. This is akin to Putin's invasion of Ukraine, fueled by Russian resources. For context, the Donbas is rich in strategic raw materials, and significant oil and gas fields were discovered off Ukraine's Black Sea coast shortly before the invasion—a clear gain Russia hoped to achieve.

Unfortunately for the mayor, the gamble does not go as planned. From the very beginning, he loses vast sums of money, just as Putin has been burning enormous amounts of Russian resources in the war. Despite occasional minor wins, it is evident to all observers that the mayor has no chance of winning this “competition.” Putin finds himself in a similar situation, with mounting losses in a conflict that was doomed from the outset.

As the mayor approaches financial ruin, he begins borrowing from anyone willing to lend—be it the restroom attendant or the waitress. Likewise, Putin is now begging North Korea for soldiers, Iran for drones, and India for computer chips. This is hardly the image of a “superpower.” Yet, to avoid admitting his failure, the mayor claims he is in a stalemate with the casino's slot machine. Observers, knowing how slot machines work, can see he is on the brink of bankruptcy.

The comparison is clear. Had the U.S. not elected Trump, the decline of Putin’s regime might have been inevitable. But instead, Trump—a known admirer of Putin—paved the way for prolonging this crisis. It’s as though the casino’s largest shareholder entered the scene, embraced the mayor, and declared: “This is a stalemate. Let’s resolve it by giving this charming man exactly what he wanted at the start—and in return, he’ll leave the slot machine alone.”

Would such a resolution be called a peace plan? History offers lessons here. Hitler justified his invasion of Czechoslovakia by claiming that ethnic Germans there were oppressed—just as Putin used similar rhetoric about Russians in Luhansk and Donetsk. Instead of standing firm against such aggression, the Allies pursued a policy of appeasement, a decision we now understand emboldened Hitler to ignite a devastating war in Europe.

We in Europe are deeply concerned about the dangerous precedent being set. Putin is being handed parts of Europe, which he will undoubtedly use to rebuild his strength. And we know what that will lead to.

Trump, however, shows no interest in curbing dictatorships. Instead, he envisions a world as a stage for super-rich autocrats, where he aspires to surpass even Putin. His disregard for democracy is evident. (For that matter, we in Europe, where democracy was born, find it difficult to understand how a system dominated by just two major parties can be called a true democracy—let alone your puzzling electoral processes.)

comments.author: @Quakeboy02
comments.text:
Everyone who pontificates about some Russian alliance with other countries to attack the United states is overlooking the key thing that prevents them from having attacking the US: The US only has 2 border counties, and neither of them is a close ally of Russia or any of the other bad actors.  It is hard, Very hard, to attack a country that is isolated on essentially all sides.  You absolutely need a beachhead. And for the United States, there is no beachhead.  To attack the US, Russia would have to first conquer either Canada or Mexico, and that's just not gonna happen.

So, why is that important?  Mostly because the greatest need for 150mm shells is for a land war.  Without a credible land enemy, most of our stockpiles are needed for exactly what they have accomplished: to help prevent friendly countries from Russian attack.  Any war waged on the United states will be waged by air and sea - and a purely air and sea attack will take a LOT of logistics just to provide replacement food and ammo for those ships and planes that are attacking us.  Yes, you could point out that Alaska is standing out like a sore thumb, but first you should probably look up the American bases, ships, and planes stationed in Alaska.  Our strongest advanced early warning systems are in Alaska.  Alaska is not vulnerable by any stretch of the imagination.

So, replacing 150mm shells is not the priority for us as it is for those we send those shells to.  Our priority must be to whittle away at Russia, through the actions of our allies, to the point that Russia is nothing more than an old bear, unable to defend itself.  Now is the perfect opportunity for the US.  Morale in Russia is probably as low as it's ever going to get.  They've let so many men out of prison to be slaughtered in Ukraine that they no longer have even a semblance of reserve troops.  At some point, they are not going to have any troops or munitions to send to any of their satellite states.  The best way to ensure that is to not only continue to support Ukraine, but to greatly increase our support.  At some point in the not so distant future, some president, perhaps Trump, will have the opportunity to end Russia as a nation - but only if we continue to increase our support of Ukraine.  We will never ever get a better chance of ending the Russian threat than we have now.

comments.text:
Things are SUDDENLY looking up for Ukraine? Are you for real? I really don't know which Ukrainian news you have been watching but all I have seen especially since 2024 is a Ukraine that is rapidly building up its own drone and missile factories and is definitely going to win this war against Russia as long as we keep helping them out. And they have done phenomenally well all during 2025 with Operation Spiderweb and the increasingly successful bombardments of russian refineries, as well as stopping any perceived advances that Putin thought his troops has made, initially in the Sumy region where they were beaten back quite conclusively in late Spring, and pushing back any attempts Putin made with his summer offensive around Pokrovsk which went nowhere, as well as the refinery attacks, the attack on the russian submarine that had been hiding in the port of Novorossiysk, and construction of the large kill zones of more than 20 kilometres deep, after having manufactured 3,200,000 million drones in 2025, which account for 90% of the russians killed each day (about 1000 per day), all of which are now verified and identified by the drone operators in order to be counted for their downing missions with their drones. Add the Flamingo cruise missile and the other Fire point missiles and drones FP1, FP2, FP5, FP7, as well as Neptune missiles and other long distance drones that can hit deep into russia and I cannot understand how any educated person could still think that russia is going to win the war. Ukraine has been winning this war since 2024 and now, given that the 90 billion Euros loan plus another 60 billion Euros in military help, Europe is no longer just helping Ukraine not lose the war but giving them the necessary financial future for 2 years that will enable them to win this war which is what Europe is now signalling they want - at last. 
But those of us who have been standing with Ukraine for the last 4 years and 62 days have known since 2024 that russia is going to lose this war and that Ukraine has the necessary homegrown weapons industry to take this war to russia and to beat them because the russian economy is going to collapse now whereas Ukraine can keep paying the salaries for the government workers, the wages for the military and can finance the production of the many missiles and drones, interceptor drones and anti aircraft protective missiles to shoot down russian drones and missiles without even having to depend on the totally unreliable Americans who just want russia to win. With the 90 billion Euro loan, Europe has moved behind Ukraine in lockstep and has given them the financial freedom to plan for two years of war with the required finances to upscale their weapons productions and keep the home lights burning as well. 
That will enable them to plan ahead their strategies without having to worry about money running out and with the knowledge that they don't need America to win the war any longer, this will give them the added motivation to go full out and slam the russians back into the stone age (at least their economy). russia is quite capable of slamming themselves back into the stone age, most of russia is still without running water, gas or bathroom facilities. You only get these amenities in the West of russia, the other parts are living like in the middle ages. It will be easy for Ukraine to break russia in two.

comments.author: @alessiop2808
comments.text:
Orban was far from being a saint, but Hungary’s veto was the only thing saving Europe from the dictatorship of a politically correct monolith, enforced by those who censor anyone with a dissenting opinion. We shall expect diplomats to actually do their jobs and prevent wars instead of triggering them, ignoring tensions until expansion occurs, and then fleeing embassies while using my tax money to perpetuate the conflict.
​Investigations and journalists have now confirmed what was already clear to those who aren’t brainwashed: it made no sense for the Russians to destroy the NordStream. Yet, this won’t open European eyes. Who truly benefits when "drones," supposedly Russian, enter NATO borders? Is it a Russia that is struggling to conclude one war, or European defense companies needing to meet shareholder expectations? Is it the USA, demanding we increase military spending, or the EU, desperate to distract us from internal crises and the German recession?
​Politics is, first and foremost, diplomacy—a concept the EU has forgotten because its citizens fail to protest. Consequently, we now pay for Russian gas sold to Indians, who sell it to Americans, who then sell it back to us at a premium. We remain idle and inept, blaming migrants while ignoring this blatant self-sabotage. NATO’s utility ended in 1991, yet its expansion continued, poking the bear for decades. If you beat a dog, you cannot be surprised when it bites, nor is the solution to beat it harder.
​Despite the 2026 election results in Hungary, the underlying issue remains: diplomacy died long ago because Europe refused to listen to Russia’s concerns regarding NATO’s proximity. You solve a war by understanding the root cause—which is NATO expansion, not just Ukraine—not by excluding dialogue and hiding the truth of what has been happening since 2014. If Europe continues to reject diplomacy, violence will remain the only answer. Something notable about that point at 4:05 about Russia being bailed out by sanction easing and the Strait of Hormuz debacle:  Ukraine's strikes on the oil export terminals in the Gulf of Finland that took out 40% of their export capacity - that was a *response* to the first sign of sanction easing, and has been reported to have functionally offset the benefits they might have gotten from the Hormuz situation.  Of course, that only works up to a point...but also Ukraine can reach all but the pacific export terminals with some version of weapon or another (and the majority of those are in easy reach in the Black Sea or the Gulf of Finland, so Ukraine can implement further "kinetic sanctions")

(Also, the internal pipelines they can't reach and the export terminal in the pacific are probably mostly China related, and Ukraine might find attacking Chinese oil import sources to be a net negative overall.  China seems like they're largely not picking sides rather than actively supporting Russia, and probably not a good idea to change that.)

comments.author: @magnvss
comments.text:
History shows this pattern repeatedly (from Vietnam to Afghanistan to the Iran‑Iraq war) where initial mobilization eventually gave way to fatigue, declining morale, and political pressure to end or scale back.

Even if Ukraine receives billions in aid, sustaining the fight depends on more than weapons and logistics. Troop stamina, civilian resilience, and political will are finite resources. When exhaustion sets in, cracks appear: desertions rise, recruitment slows, and public support wanes. That’s why long wars often shift from decisive battles to attrition, and why leaders must balance external aid with the internal human cost.

Money can buy material stuff, but it cannot indefinitely buy morale. The real question becomes whether Ukraine can translate external support into a sustainable strategy before exhaustion erodes its capacity.

nobody who was paying serious attention thought that Russian military forces were doing well last year.

The escalation to the point of War has been happening for 7 years. There is not One Political leader that was not aware of what was proposed in the MINSK II 2015 Agreement. These were the 13 points (many which benefit as an independent country); 

Immediate, comprehensive ceasefire. 
Withdrawal of heavy weapons by both sides. 
OSCE monitoring (Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe). 
Dialogue on interim self-government for Donetsk and Luhansk, in accordance with Ukrainian law, and acknowledgement of special status by parliament. 
Pardon, amnesty for fighters. Exchange of hostages, prisoners. 
Humanitarian assistance. Resumption of socioeconomic ties, including pensions.
 Ukraine to restore control of state border. 
Withdrawal of foreign armed formations, military equipment, mercenaries. 
Constitutional reform in Ukraine including decentralisation, with specific mention of Donetsk and Luhansk. 
Elections in Donetsk and Luhansk. Intensify Trilateral Contact Group’s work including representatives of Russia, Ukraine and OSCE (Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe).

comments.author: @TheBerendir
comments.text:
I remember watching this and believing the idea that the West shouldn’t provoke the “mighty Russian bear.” I thought of it as the second most powerful military in the world, and even assumed Putin was someone we could work with. At the time, I couldn’t even point out Ukraine on a map—I thought Chornobyl was in Russia.
Then in February 2022, I was convinced Kyiv would fall within three days. I couldn’t have been more wrong. Russia’s military performance has been deeply underwhelming; without nuclear weapons, its power looks far less formidable than many believed. I also remember how seriously I took its repeated “red lines” and the fear of nuclear escalation.
Now, nearly three years into learning Ukrainian, I’m constantly impressed by the resilience and character of its people. While it may not happen, I believe stronger, more decisive support would be justified to help bring an end to this invasion. Все буде Україні 🇬🇧💙🇺🇦👍

comments.author: @A_friend_of_Aristotle
comments.text:
Within 6 months 30% of these men will be dead or crippled, meaning unable to live a meaningful, productive life in Russia. They will be the next round of Russian "Heroes" and a gruesome reminder of Putin's non-war invasion of Ukraine. Russian's complain so much about the hardships they go through...at what point do they understand that its self-inflicted? I won't shed a tear for the majority of these men...they will follow the orders of the authorities, and be largely indifferent to the danger or the facts staring them right in the face. They have been living their lives with *_indifference,_* and that's how they are going to die.

The lucky ones will die in a blinding flash, probably ripped to shreds in some farmers field.

The unlucky ones will bleed out slowly after being abandoned on the battle field, or manage to drag themselves back to base where they'll be shot for "cowardice."

The most unlucky of the horde are the ones who will die years later from starvation...left on the streets like animals by a culture of - and led by - *_indifferent people._*

comments.author: @eyeless4464
comments.text: At least two million children have died in the last 10 years as a result of wars started by adults, whether they were civilian targets or whether they were killed in combat as child soldiers. The number of children wounded or disabled is three times larger, and there are even more suffering from sicknesses, malnutrition, sexual violence, and the hardships of flight. Countless children have been confronted with the anguish of losing their home, their belongings, and those close to them. In such conditions, practically all the necessary constants for child development are seriously disrupted, and the psychological damages of armed conflicts are incalculable.  Children must be supported in healing from the effect of toxic stress to break the cycle of violence in which the next generation struggles to rebuild society after the trauma of war. But a focus on the short term effect of war means scant attention has been paid to longer term mental health support. A cohesive effort is needed among policy makers, humanitarian agencies, and health services in the region to increase resilience and prevent escalation in mental and physical health problems, and to advocate for security and health.

In 1933 the US Federal Government forced its citizens to 'turn in' all their gold via Executive Order #6102, which led to the enactment of the Gold Reserve Act of 1934. This theft was to 'protect the currency system' of The Federal Reserve Bank(which is a privately owned bank, not even part of the US Government). In 1963 President Kennedy had a solution to return monetary control and printing power back to the Federal Government from the privately owned Federal Reserve Bank with Executive Order #11110 but was killed just 3 months after signing this EO, which was then reversed after his death. Then in 1971 the USD loses all its intrinsic backing when Nixon terminated the gold/dollar convertibility, creating the current 'fiat' note. Federal Reserve currency has been losing value ever since...

Since WWII ended, America has instigated over 200 armed conflicts, most of which to protect the stability of the dollar. A review over just the last 20 years; Iraq stopped accepting USD for oil in 2000 and was invaded in 2001. Libya stopped accepting USD for oil in 2011 and created a gold backed currency, they get invaded same year, Venezuela stops accepting USD for oil in 2017 and tried creating a gold and oil backed currency, a coup was attempted and failed. Iran actually stopped accepting USD for oil in 2008 and completely banned the Dollar in 2017 which spawned CIA backed protest and US sanctions. Russia and China agreed to a deal in 2019 to bypass the SWIFT payment system with China's new crypto based financial clearing system(CIPS) and trade without the dollar. It's all about maintaining the supremacy of the Dollar, a fiat currency stabilized through Aid(gving USD to other nations in return for accepting it as having value) and War.

Geschichte sachlich wiedergegeben:
1989-1991
Der Russe erlaubt die Abschaffung der Demarkationslinie zwischen Ost - und Westdeutschland und löst den Warschauer Pakt auf, zieht seine Truppen aus Osteuropa ab und vertraut auf die mündliche Zusage von Genscher und Baker, dass die ehemaligen Warschauer Pakt Mitglieder nicht der NATO beitreten. Die amerikanischen Truppen bleiben.
Bis 2004 sind dann fast alle Warschauer Pakt-Mitglieder der NATO beigetreten. Der Russe bleibt ruhig.
2001
Putin macht dem Westen im Deutschen Bundestag ein Angebot für eine enge Partnerschaft, um die Spaltung der Vergangenheit zu überwinden. Der Amerikaner verbietet das. Es gibt mit Weißrussland, der Ukraine und den 3 baltischen Staaten einen Puffer zwischen der Nato und Russland.
2004
die baltischen Staaten werden Mitglieder der NATO. Eine erste Verletzung der Pufferzone. Der Russe bleibt ruhig.
2014
putscht der Westen die Russland freundliche Regierung der Ukraine aus dem Amt und installiert eine USA freundliche Regierung. Im gleichen Moment beginnen die USA, allen voran Hunter Biden, Monsanto, Black Water und US-Militärberater, die Ukraine zu infiltrieren und eine Nato Mitgliedschaft in Aussicht zu stellen. Die zweite Verletzung der Pufferzone fand statt.
2014
Durch den Putsch der US-Amerikaner genötigt, gab es eine friedliche Besetzung der Krim. Es fiel entgegen aller anderen Darstellungen kein einziger Schuss, niemand wurde getötet. Außer ein paar Krimtataren sind bis heute 90 % der Krim Einwohner mit diesem Vorgehen einverstanden gewesen.
2020
Nach dem Vorbild von 2014 in der Ukraine versucht der Westen nun auch in Weißrussland die Regierung zu stürzen, was misslingt, aber es war der dritte Angriff auf die Pufferzone zwischen der Nato und Russland. Der Russe bleibt ruhig.
2022
Putin fordert ein letztes Mal eine Garantie, dass die Ukraine kein Mitglied der NATO wird und Donezk und Lugansk sich weitgehend auf dem Gebiet der Ukraine selbst verwalten dürfen. Dies wird von den USA abgelehnt.
2022
Der Russe marschiert in der Ukraine ein. Um den Krieg zu beenden, verlangt Putin eine Garantie der Neutralität und eine Entmilitarisierung der Ukraine, eine Anerkennung von Donezk und Lugansk als Volksrepubliken und eine Anerkennung der Krim als russisches Hoheitsgebiet. comments.text: My parents worked their whole life to build a house, to raise me in my country , to leave me and my future kids property and safety. All my life I remember them working hard and always staying positive. We traveled to Crimea every summer, I stayed there with my mom for months, I went to summer camps there and I went camping for weeks. We never hated Russians, we thought we were brothers and friends. Then after 2014, everything changed. My dad became more nervous and angry, he was keeping track of all the political movements just to feel like he controls anything at least. We were afraid to make any move in our own country, build anything at all, spend any money and invest in anything. About a year ago my parents basically said 'screw it! We're tired of being afraid' and bought 2 apartments to rent in the future in a community that was building. Now, it all doesn't matter anymore, we don't even talk about those apartments, because our lives are the priority. But, I just want to let you know what it's like living in a country where you're afraid to make any movement. Our family doesn't have it bad, we're lucky to have each other, and my heart breaks for my people who lost someone, or young mothers who have to leave the country with newborns... think about it? By themselves, because men can't eave. There's so much to say, and I just go from one thought to another... but honestly... war is the worst thing that can happen. In a way, I'm happy I got to experience smth like that because if all of this ends, I will live forever happy just when I know what happens tomorrow and that I'm in charge of my own life. I love my family, I hope we will all stay safe in the end. No money matters now

comments.author: @lili77887
comments.text:
Over the past few days, I have been watching my friends around the world change their Instagram and Facebook avatars to Ukrainian flags.
  Those who live in America or Europe do so because they have succumbed to the rhetoric of the mainstream media without even knowing how to locate Ukraine on a map.
  But there were also Russians who did the same.  In truth, most of them live abroad.  When the war started, they changed their avatar with the words: "I woke up with a sense of shame."
  It is strange that these same people have not felt shame for the last 8 years, while children, women and the elderly are dying in the Donbass.
  I am a Serbian and survived the NATO bombing in 1999.  For almost three months I hid from the bombs, fearing for myself and the lives of my loved ones.
  That is why I have the right to say that war is the most terrible thing in the world.  Any normal person would condemn any form of violence.
  Do you really think that those who are at the head of the Russian state are celebrating because they were forced to go to war?  That they did it because of their imperialist ambitions?
  No friends.  Russia endured.  For the past 8 years, Russia has not ceased to insist on the observance of the Minsk negotiations, which Kyiv refused to comply with.
  And they had a lot of patience while Ukrainians were killing Russians in a neighboring country.
  At the same time, more and more Nazi movements were organized in Ukraine, they did everything possible to kill the memory of the common Soviet past.  They destroyed monuments, historical symbols and forbade speaking Russian.
  The West has long been trying in every possible way to provoke the Russians to attack Ukraine.  There has been talk of an "imminent attack" for months now.
  Hence the panic was born.  And not in Russia, which has already denied these versions of bad intentions many times.
  This caused a panic in Ukraine, which continued to bomb Donbass day after day, feeling strong, thinking that NATO would turn its back on it.
  Now their president says they feel lonely.  Surprise?
  It is surprising that they are now surprised by the reaction of Russia.  They have been provoking one of the most powerful countries in the world for years, not respecting agreements, breaking promises.  And now they're surprised?
  The NATO alliance promised not to expand and did so openly.  Every day it is getting closer and closer to the borders of Russia.
  It's time for them to say a lot.  And yes, it hurts.  Russia is trying to stop NATO and their imperialist future.
  Many of those who are now shouting “no war” are the same people who for years promoted NATO expansion, organized the bloody revolution in Kyiv, armed and continue to arm Ukraine.
  In the end, it looks like this: this action was inevitable, for someone it was inevitable, but it is also a fact that after this February 24, the balance of power in the world will never be the same.
  Elena Milincic's video has been watched by millions of people around the world - there are almost 800,000 views on just one  channel: tiktok
